body.text: A House for a God

Where a Greek temple was a beautiful box to shelter a statue and a Roman one a civic monument, the Hindu temple — the **mandir** — is something stranger and more inward. It is conceived as the literal dwelling-place of a deity, who is invited to inhabit the consecrated image within, and as a model of the whole cosmos rendered in stone. To enter it is to make a journey from the ordinary world toward the centre of all things.

The plan follows an ancient diagram, the **vastu-purusha-mandala**, a sacred grid laid over the ground that aligns the building with the cardinal directions and the order of the universe. At the heart of the temple lies its most important and least adorned space: the **garbhagriha**, the "womb-chamber," a small, dark, windowless cell that houses the central image of the god. Above it rises the tower — the curving **shikhara** of the north or the stepped, pyramidal **vimana** of the south — a man-made mountain pointing the eye and the soul upward toward heaven.

body.title: A journey from light into dark
body.text: The genius of the temple is its choreography of movement. The worshipper passes from the dazzling, crowded, sunlit exterior — its walls alive with carvings of gods, dancers, animals and lovers, the whole teeming abundance of creation — through a series of ever-darker, ever-quieter halls, until reaching the tiny, silent, lamp-lit cell at the centre, where there is nothing but the image of the god and the self that has come to meet it. The architecture itself enacts the inward turn of [Hindu philosophy](/article/hindu-philosophy): from the many to the one, from the outward world to the still point within.
Two Great Styles

Over the centuries Hindu architecture flowered into two great regional traditions. In the north arose the **Nagara** style, its temples crowned by a single soaring, beehive-curved **shikhara**, clustered sometimes with smaller echoing towers — as at the exquisite, sensuous shrines of **Khajuraho** or the great sun-temple of **Konark**, carved as a colossal chariot drawn by stone horses. In the south flourished the **Dravidian** style, with its stepped pyramidal towers over the sanctum and, later, its astonishing **gopurams** — the immense gateway-towers, encrusted from base to summit with brightly painted figures, that loom over the temple-cities of the Tamil south.

The high-water mark of the southern tradition came under the **Chola** dynasty, whose kings between roughly 900 and 1200 CE poured the wealth of a maritime empire into stone. The **Brihadisvara Temple** at Thanjavur, completed around 1010 CE, raised a tower more than sixty metres high, capped by a single dome-stone of many tonnes that engineers still marvel at — a feat of lifting and balancing to rank with the [pyramids of Giza](/article/pyramids-of-giza) or the vaulting of [Roman engineering](/article/roman-engineering).
The temple is the body of God made visible, that those who cannot see the formless may worship the form.
The Engineering of Awe

The ambition of these buildings demanded extraordinary craft. Temples were raised almost entirely without mortar, from precisely cut granite and sandstone blocks locked together by their own weight and shape, much as the [builders of Greece](/article/greek-architecture) fitted their marble. Massive stones were hauled up long earthen ramps; towers were corbelled inward course by course until they met at the summit. Every surface was then given over to the sculptor, so that the structure and its decoration became inseparable — the building is the carving, and the carving is theology.

For the figures that swarm across temple walls are not ornament for its own sake. They form a vast illustrated scripture: the avatars of [Vishnu](/article/hindu-gods), the cosmic dance of Shiva, scenes from the [Ramayana](/article/ramayana) and the [Mahabharata](/article/mahabharata), the gods and goddesses in their full multiplicity. A temple was a book the unlettered could read, a sermon in stone available to anyone who walked its corridors.

body.title: Temples that never closed
body.text: Unlike the silent ruins of the ancient Mediterranean, India's great temples are mostly living shrines still. At Thanjavur, at Madurai, at Tirupati, the lamps are still lit, the bells still ring, and the same rites are performed that were performed a thousand years ago. Some draw more pilgrims in a year than any building on Earth. They are not monuments to a vanished faith but working houses of a god still believed to be at home.
Stone Made Sacred

The Hindu temple is one of humanity's great architectural achievements — comparable in ambition to the cathedrals of Europe or the pyramids of Egypt, yet built on an entirely different principle. It does not gather a crowd to face an altar; it draws the single soul inward to meet the divine in darkness and silence. In its rising tower it images the cosmic mountain at the centre of the world; in its sculpted walls it tells the whole story of the gods; and in its sequence of chambers it teaches, in stone, the central lesson of the tradition that built it — that the way to the infinite leads not outward but in.

title: Hinduism
lede: Hinduism is the oldest living religion on Earth — not a single founder's creed but a vast, woven inheritance of scripture, ritual, philosophy and story that has grown on the Indian subcontinent for more than three thousand years. Its followers call it sanatana dharma, the eternal law, and within its boundless variety lies room for one supreme reality and a thousand million faces of the divine.

body.text: A Religion Without a Beginning

Most faiths can name a founder and a moment of revelation. Hinduism can name neither. It has no single prophet, no one book, no central authority and no creed that every believer must accept. Instead it is a confluence — the slow merging of the rituals of the **Vedic** peoples who composed sacred hymns more than three thousand years ago with the older devotions of the [Indus Valley](/article/hinduism) and the countless local traditions of the subcontinent. The very word *Hindu* began as a geographic label, the Persian name for the people who lived beyond the river Sindhu, the Indus. Only much later did it come to name a religion.

Its followers more often call it **sanatana dharma** — the eternal way, or eternal law. The phrase captures something essential. Hinduism is less a set of doctrines to be believed than an order to be lived: a *dharma*, a right way of acting, that holds the cosmos and the conscience together. Within that order a worshipper may be a strict philosopher who sees the divine as a single formless absolute, or a villager who garlands a much-loved local goddess, and both are wholly Hindu.

body.title: One reality, many names
body.text: The most quoted line in all the [Vedas](/article/vedas) reads: *"Truth is one; the wise call it by many names."* This single idea — that the one supreme reality, **Brahman**, can be approached through countless gods, images and paths — is the key that unlocks Hinduism's astonishing variety. It is not many religions wearing one name, but one vision wearing many faces.
The Great Ideas

Beneath the variety run a few currents shared across almost every Hindu tradition. The first is **Brahman**, the ultimate reality underlying all things, and the **atman**, the innermost self — which the [Upanishads](/article/hindu-philosophy) boldly declare to be one and the same. To know, in the depths of meditation, that *atman is Brahman* is to glimpse the secret of existence.

The second is the great wheel of **samsara**, the cycle of birth, death and rebirth through which every soul passes, life after life. What carries a soul forward is **karma** — the moral law of cause and effect, by which every action ripens into a consequence. To live well is to sow well. The aim of the whole spiritual life is finally **moksha**: liberation, the release of the soul from the wheel and its reunion with the infinite.
You are what your deep, driving desire is. As your desire is, so is your will. As your will is, so is your deed. As your deed is, so is your destiny.
That passage from the Upanishads turns the entire moral universe on the quiet axis of the human heart.
Many Paths Up One Mountain

Because the goal is liberation and the seekers are infinitely various, Hinduism offers not one road but several. The **Bhagavad Gita**, the most beloved of all Hindu scriptures, sets out three great paths that the divine charioteer Krishna teaches to the warrior Arjuna. There is the path of **action** done without selfish attachment to its fruits; the path of **knowledge**, the philosopher's ascent through wisdom; and the path of **devotion**, *bhakti*, the loving surrender of the heart to a chosen form of God. A fourth, the path of disciplined practice through [yoga and meditation](/article/yoga-and-meditation), threads through them all.

body.title: A living tradition, not a relic
body.text: Unlike the religions of [ancient Egypt](/article/egyptian-religion) or [Rome](/article/roman-religion), which we study only through ruins and texts, Hinduism is fiercely alive. Its hymns are still chanted, its festivals still light up cities, its temples still crowded. Roughly **1.2 billion people** — about one in seven humans alive — follow it today, the third-largest religion on Earth and the only one of the ancient world's great faiths still practised by the people who first composed its scriptures.
A Civilisation of the Spirit

To follow Hinduism through history is to follow much of the history of India itself. From the sacrifices of the Vedic age to the soaring metaphysics of the Upanishads, from the world-spanning epics of the [Mahabharata](/article/mahabharata) and the [Ramayana](/article/ramayana) to the towering [temples](/article/hindu-temples) of the medieval south and the ecstatic devotional movements that swept the land, the tradition has continually renewed itself without ever discarding its past. The oldest layer and the newest still sit side by side.

What follows in this cluster is a journey through that inheritance: the scriptures that began it, the gods who fill it, the epics that taught it, the philosophy that deepened it, the temples that housed it, the disciplines that train the body and mind toward it, and finally the breathtaking vision of time and cosmos that frames it all. It is the story of how one of humanity's oldest peoples set out to map the whole of reality — and never stopped.

title: Islam
lede: Islam is the faith of some 1.9 billion people — nearly a quarter of humanity — who hold that there is no god but God and that Muhammad is His messenger. Born in the deserts of seventh-century Arabia and carried within a single century from Spain to the Indus, it is at once the youngest of the great world religions and the heir of the oldest, claiming the same God worshipped by Abraham, Moses and Jesus. A religion of stark monotheism and total surrender, it shaped a civilization that preserved the learning of Greece, mapped the heavens, and built some of the most beautiful sacred spaces on Earth.

maps.title: From Mecca to the Ends of the Earth
maps.caption: A faith born in the Arabian towns of Mecca and Medina spread within a century across the Near East, North Africa and Spain, and in time to West Africa, Central Asia and the islands of Southeast Asia.

images.title: The Kaaba at Mecca
images.caption: The cube-shaped House of God toward which a quarter of humanity turns in prayer five times a day — the focal point of the hajj pilgrimage and the heart of the Muslim world.

images.title: The word Allah in calligraphy
images.caption: Because Islam discourages depicting the divine, the written word of God became its supreme visual art — the name of God rendered in flowing Arabic script.

videos.caption: How a message proclaimed by a merchant of Mecca became the faith of nearly two billion people across every continent.

body.text: A Word Meaning Surrender

The Arabic word *islam* means **submission** — the willing surrender of the whole self to the will of the one God, **Allah**, a name that means simply "the God." One who makes that surrender is a *muslim*, "one who submits." At the centre of the faith stands a single sentence, the **shahada**: *There is no god but God, and Muhammad is the messenger of God.* To say these words sincerely is to become a Muslim. Everything else in the religion flows from that stark and uncompromising declaration of the oneness of God.

That oneness — *tawhid* — is the beating heart of Islam. God is utterly singular, without partner, without offspring, without image. He is the creator of all that is, the merciful and the just, nearer to a person, the [Quran](/article/the-quran) says, than their own jugular vein. To associate anything else with Him — to worship an idol, to divide Him into persons, to make any creature His equal — is *shirk*, the one sin the tradition holds beyond easy forgiveness. Where [Christianity](/article/christianity) confesses one God in three persons and [Hinduism](/article/hinduism) sees the divine in a thousand forms, Islam insists on an absolute, undivided unity.

body.title: The youngest faith, the oldest claim
body.text: Islam does not present itself as a new religion at all, but as the final restoration of the oldest one. It teaches that God sent the same message through a long line of prophets — Adam, Noah, Abraham, Moses, Jesus and many more — and that humanity repeatedly forgot or corrupted it. Muhammad is held to be the *seal of the prophets*, the last in that line, through whom the message was given a final, perfect and unalterable form. To a Muslim, Abraham was a Muslim, and so was Jesus: not founders of rival faiths but earlier messengers of the same eternal truth.
The Book and the Prophet

Islam rests on two foundations. The first is the [Quran](/article/the-quran), which Muslims hold to be the literal, uncreated speech of God, revealed to [Muhammad](/article/muhammad) through the angel Gabriel over twenty-three years and preserved without change ever since. It is not a book *about* God in the way the [Bible](/article/the-bible) is a library of human testimony; it is understood as God's own words, which is why it is recited in the original Arabic the world over and why its beauty of sound is itself considered a miracle.

The second foundation is the example of the Prophet himself — his sayings and deeds, gathered as the **hadith** and woven together into the **sunna**, the path of the Prophet. Where the Quran is silent or brief, the remembered life of Muhammad guides the believer. From the Quran and the sunna the scholars of Islam built the **sharia**, a comprehensive way of life that reaches from prayer and pilgrimage to commerce, marriage and the conduct of the state.
God: there is no god but Him, the Ever Living, the Ever Watchful. Neither slumber nor sleep overtakes Him. All that is in the heavens and the earth belongs to Him.
That passage, the famous "Throne Verse," captures the Quranic vision of a God boundless and sovereign over all creation — the God to whom the Muslim surrenders.
A Religion You Practise

Islam is less a set of doctrines to be believed than a life to be lived, and its daily shape is given by the [Five Pillars](/article/five-pillars) — the testimony of faith, the five daily prayers, the giving of alms, the fast of Ramadan, and the pilgrimage to Mecca. These are not optional devotions but the very frame of a Muslim life, binding the believer five times a day to the rhythm of prayer and once a year, if they are able, into the vast white-robed throng circling the Kaaba.

body.title: One faith, many houses
body.text: Like every great tradition, Islam is not a single monolith. Its largest division, between [Sunni and Shia](/article/sunni-shia-divide), opened within a generation of the Prophet's death over the question of who should lead the community. Alongside them runs the mystical current of **Sufism**, which seeks not law alone but the direct experience of God's love, echoing the contemplative paths of [Christian mystics](/article/christian-mysticism) and the yogis of [India](/article/hinduism). From the deserts of Arabia to the rice fields of Indonesia, Islam has taken on the colours of every culture it touched while holding fast to the one creed at its core.
The Civilization It Built

Within a hundred years of Muhammad's death in 632, Arab armies carrying the new faith had overthrown the Persian Empire, stripped the Eastern Roman Empire of its richest provinces, and ruled an arc of territory larger than Rome at its height. But the deeper conquest was of the mind. While much of Christian Europe forgot the learning of antiquity after the [fall of Rome](/article/fall-of-rome), the scholars of the [Islamic Golden Age](/article/islamic-golden-age) gathered, translated and surpassed the science and philosophy of [Greece](/article/greek-philosophy), India and Persia, giving the world algebra, advances in medicine and astronomy, and the very numerals these words are written beside.
The Story This Cluster Tells

What follows is a journey through that inheritance. We begin with the life of [Muhammad](/article/muhammad) himself, then turn to the [Quran](/article/the-quran) revealed through him and the [Five Pillars](/article/five-pillars) that frame a Muslim life. We follow the faith into its dazzling [Golden Age](/article/islamic-golden-age) of learning, into the searching tradition of [Islamic philosophy](/article/islamic-philosophy), and into the soaring [architecture](/article/islamic-architecture) of dome and minaret. We trace the great [Sunni–Shia divide](/article/sunni-shia-divide) that runs through Muslim history, and end with [Islam today](/article/islam-today), a faith of nearly two billion souls, the fastest-growing religion on Earth and the spiritual home of one person in four.

title: Islam Today
lede: With some 1.9 billion adherents — nearly a quarter of humanity — Islam is the second-largest religion on Earth and, by most measures, the fastest-growing. Yet the heart of the Muslim world is no longer only the Arab lands of its birth; today the largest Muslim populations live in South and Southeast Asia, and Islam is woven into the life of every continent. From the great pilgrim cities to the mosques of Europe and the Americas, the faith founded in seventh-century Arabia enters its fifteenth century as a truly global religion, diverse, dynamic and young.

maps.title: The Muslim World Today
maps.caption: From Morocco to Indonesia stretches a broad band of Muslim-majority nations, while the largest single populations live in Indonesia, Pakistan, India and Bangladesh — far from the Arab heartland of Islam's birth.
maps.region: Worldwide, centred on Asia and Africa

images.title: A modern hajj
images.caption: Each year some two million pilgrims from every nation gather at Mecca — one of the largest peaceful assemblies of human beings on the planet.

images.title: A mosque in a Western city
images.caption: From London to Toronto, growing Muslim communities have made Islam a visible part of the religious landscape of the West.

citations.label: Pew Research Center — The Future of World Religions

body.text: A Quarter of Humanity

Islam today counts some **1.9 billion** followers — close to a quarter of all the people alive. Only [Christianity](/article/christianity-today) has more adherents, and on present trends the gap is closing fast: with a young population and high birth rates across much of the Muslim world, Islam is the fastest-growing major religion on Earth, and demographers project that the number of Muslims may roughly equal the number of Christians before the century is out. It is a faith not in retreat but in expansion, entering its fifteenth century with extraordinary vitality.

Perhaps the most surprising fact about the Muslim world is where it lies. Although Islam was born among the Arabs and the [Quran](/article/the-quran) was revealed in Arabic, Arabs today make up only about one in five of the world's Muslims. The largest Muslim populations live far to the east: **Indonesia** is the most populous Muslim nation on Earth, followed by **Pakistan**, **India** and **Bangladesh**. The centre of gravity of the faith has long since shifted from the deserts of its birth to the teeming nations of South and Southeast Asia — and Islam is now woven into the life of Africa, Europe and the Americas as well.

body.title: A young and global faith
body.text: The single most important fact about Islam's future is its youth. Across the Muslim world the median age is strikingly low, and a faith with a young population is a growing one. This demographic energy, combined with the steady spread of Islam in sub-Saharan Africa, means that the twenty-first century will likely see the Muslim share of humanity rise faster than that of any other tradition. Like [Hinduism](/article/hinduism) and Christianity, Islam remains one of the great living forces shaping the spiritual life of billions — but it is, in a sense, the youngest and the fastest-rising of them all.
Many Ways of Being Muslim

The Muslim world is no monolith. Beyond the great [Sunni–Shia divide](/article/sunni-shia-divide), Islam is lived in a thousand ways across its vast geography. In the markets of Cairo, the rice fields of Java, the steppes of Central Asia and the suburbs of Paris, the same Five Pillars frame lives shaped by utterly different cultures, languages and histories. There are Muslims who read their faith in conservative and traditional terms, and Muslims who embrace thoroughly modern and liberal interpretations; there are the inward, ecstatic devotions of the **Sufi** orders and the strict legalism of reform movements; there are quiet secular Muslims and fervent activists.

This diversity has been the source of vigorous debate within Islam over how the faith should meet the modern world — questions of law and democracy, of the role of women, of science and tradition, of how a revelation given in seventh-century Arabia should guide life in the twenty-first century. These are live and contested conversations, carried on by Muslims themselves across the whole spectrum of opinion, much as every great tradition wrestles in each age with how to be faithful to its founding vision under new conditions.
O humankind, We created you from a single pair of a male and a female, and made you into nations and tribes, that you may know one another.

That verse from the Quran, often quoted today, speaks to a faith that has always crossed the lines of race and nation — and that now, more than ever, gathers peoples of every continent into a single community.
The Pilgrim City and the Global Diaspora

Nothing displays the global reach of Islam more vividly than the annual **hajj**. Each year some two million pilgrims, drawn from virtually every country on Earth, converge on Mecca to perform the rites that [Muhammad](/article/muhammad) established — making it one of the largest peaceful gatherings of human beings anywhere on the planet. In the white-robed throng circling the Kaaba, the nationalities and tongues of the whole world dissolve for a few days into a single act of worship, an image of unity that has moved believers for fourteen centuries.

Beyond the heartlands, Islam is now a permanent and growing presence in the West. Migration over the past century has built substantial Muslim communities across Europe, North America and beyond, where mosques have become part of the urban landscape and Muslims contribute to every walk of public life. This diaspora has made Islam, for the first time, a near-universal religion in the literal sense — present in significant numbers on every inhabited continent.

body.title: A faith and its perceptions
body.text: In recent decades Islam has often been viewed in the West through the narrow and distorting lens of conflict and a handful of violent extremist movements. Yet such movements represent a tiny fraction of the world's Muslims, the overwhelming majority of whom live ordinary lives of faith, family and work, and many of whom are themselves the principal victims of extremist violence. To understand Islam today is to look past the headlines to the vast, varied and largely peaceful reality of nearly two billion believers — the same faith that once lit the lamps of the [Golden Age](/article/islamic-golden-age) of learning, lived out across the whole face of the Earth.
Into the Fifteenth Century

By the Islamic calendar, which counts from the Hijra of 622, the faith has now passed into its fifteenth century. It does so as a religion of remarkable energy — young, growing, global, and as deeply rooted in the daily lives of its people as any tradition on Earth. The five daily prayers still turn nearly two billion faces toward Mecca; the fast of Ramadan still empties the streets at dusk across half the world; the pilgrim throng still circles the ancient shrine. From a message proclaimed by a single man in the hills above Mecca, Islam has become the spiritual home of one person in four — and its story, far from finished, is still being written across every continent of the world.

title: Islamic Architecture
eyebrow: Geometry in the service of God
lede: From the great mosque of Córdoba to the Taj Mahal, Islamic architecture has given the world some of its most beautiful buildings — spaces of dome and arch, courtyard and fountain, where light, water and infinite geometric pattern conspire to lift the heart toward God. Forbidden to depict the divine in image, Muslim builders made of abstraction itself a supreme art, covering their walls in flowing calligraphy and endlessly repeating patterns that seem to hint at the boundlessness of the One they could not show.

maps.caption: From the Alhambra in Spain to the Dome of the Rock in Jerusalem, the mosques of Istanbul and Isfahan, and the Mughal monuments of India — a shared architectural language stretched across half the world.

images.caption: In the palaces of Granada, water, shade and intricate stucco create an earthly image of the paradise gardens promised in the Quran.

images.title: The Taj Mahal
images.caption: The Mughal emperor Shah Jahan's marble mausoleum for his wife — the most famous building of the Islamic world, where Persian, Indian and Islamic traditions meet.

videos.title: The Art of the Infinite
videos.caption: How Muslim builders turned geometry, calligraphy and light into a sacred art without ever depicting the divine.

citations.label: The Metropolitan Museum of Art — Islamic Art

body.text: Building for the One Who Cannot Be Seen

Every sacred architecture answers to the god it serves. The temples of [India](/article/hindu-temples) house the carved image of the deity in a dark inner chamber; the churches of [Christendom](/article/christian-art) fill their walls with the painted faces of Christ and the saints. But Islam holds the oneness of God to be beyond all image and likeness, and its tradition strongly discourages depicting living forms in sacred space, lest they tempt the worshipper toward idolatry. This single principle shaped everything. Denied the human figure, Muslim artists poured their genius into other channels — into geometry, calligraphy, light and space — and made of these an art of overwhelming beauty.

The result is an architecture of abstraction. Where a cathedral overwhelms with the drama of the human and divine made visible, a great mosque overwhelms with pattern and proportion, with the play of light through screens, with the endless repetition of geometric forms that seem to have no beginning and no end. The very abstraction becomes a theology: a building that points beyond every image to the formless, infinite God.

body.title: The art of infinity
body.text: The intricate star-patterns and interlacing **arabesques** that cover Islamic buildings are not mere decoration. Built from geometry — circles divided and redivided, polygons interlocking without end — they can in principle be extended forever in every direction. Many scholars read them as a meditation on the infinite: the worshipper's eye, finding no centre and no edge, no figure to rest upon, is drawn toward the boundlessness of God Himself. The same mathematical genius that produced [algebra](/article/islamic-golden-age) in the Golden Age here became a spiritual art, turning the cold certainties of geometry into a vision of the divine.
The Mosque and Its Elements

At the heart of the tradition stands the **mosque** — *masjid*, "place of prostration." Its form grew from the simplest of origins: the courtyard of [Muhammad's](/article/muhammad) own house in Medina, where the first community gathered to pray. From that humble model developed a set of elements found in mosques the world over. A spacious **prayer hall** holds the rows of worshippers; a niche in one wall, the **mihrab**, marks the *qibla*, the direction of Mecca toward which all must face; a raised pulpit, the **minbar**, serves the Friday sermon. Outside, a **courtyard** with a fountain provides water for the ritual washing before prayer, and the slender tower of the **minaret** rises above the city, from which the call to prayer once rang out by the human voice alone.

Above many great mosques floats the **dome**, that most resonant of forms, an image of the vault of heaven arching over the gathered faithful. Here the Muslim builders learned from those who came before. From the engineers of [Rome](/article/roman-engineering) and the Christian Byzantines they took the science of the great dome and the round arch; from [Greek](/article/greek-architecture) and Persian building they took columns and proportion. But they transformed what they borrowed, raising the pointed arch and the horseshoe arch, and crowning interiors with the **muqarnas** — cascading honeycombs of vaulting like frozen stalactites, dissolving solid stone into shimmering pattern.
God is beautiful, and He loves beauty.
That saying of the Prophet became a charter for the Islamic arts — the conviction that to make something beautiful is itself a way of honouring God.
Gardens, Water and the Image of Paradise

Born in the parched lands of Arabia and Persia, Islamic architecture made water and shade into the very image of the divine reward. The [Quran](/article/the-quran) describes paradise again and again as a garden watered by flowing streams, and Muslim builders set out to make an echo of it on Earth. The classic Islamic garden is divided by four channels of running water into quarters — the *chahar bagh*, a fourfold paradise garden — cool, green and ordered against the heat and chaos outside. In the palace of the **Alhambra** in Granada, courtyards of still pools and trickling fountains create a hushed, otherworldly calm, an earthly foretaste of the gardens promised to the faithful.
A Style That Spanned the World

As Islam spread, its architecture absorbed the genius of every land it reached and gave back a shared vocabulary recognisable from Spain to India. In Jerusalem rose the **Dome of the Rock**, built around 691, the oldest great monument of the faith, its golden dome and patterned tilework set above the rock from which Muslims believe Muhammad ascended through the heavens. In Ottoman Istanbul, the architect Sinan raised vast domed mosques that answered the Byzantine Hagia Sophia. In Safavid Persia, the city of Isfahan glowed with mosques of turquoise tile. And in Mughal **India**, where Islam met the building traditions of [the Hindu world](/article/hindu-temples), the emperor Shah Jahan raised the **Taj Mahal** — a marble tomb for his beloved wife, perfectly symmetrical, mirrored in a long reflecting pool, perhaps the most beloved building on Earth.

body.title: Calligraphy, the queen of arts
body.text: If geometry is the body of Islamic art, calligraphy is its soul. Because the [Quran](/article/the-quran) is held to be the literal word of God, the writing of that word became the highest of all the visual arts. Verses flow in elegant Arabic script across the portals and domes of mosques, around the rims of bowls, along the blades of swords — so that the believer is surrounded not by images of the sacred but by the sacred words themselves. In a faith without icons, the written word of God became the supreme thing to behold.
The Beauty That Points Beyond Itself

To stand in the great mosque of Córdoba, beneath its forest of red-and-white striped arches receding into shadow, or in the tiled courtyards of Isfahan, or before the floating marble of the Taj Mahal, is to feel what Islamic architecture set out to achieve. It does not tell stories or show faces. It works on the soul through proportion and pattern, through light and water and the hush of ordered space, drawing the mind away from the particular and the visible toward the one, infinite, unseen God. In refusing to picture the divine, the builders of Islam found another way to make the heart aware of it — and left the world some of the most serenely beautiful spaces human hands have ever raised.

title: Islamic Philosophy
lede: When the reasoning of ancient Greece flowed into the Muslim world, it met a faith founded on revelation, and from that meeting came one of the richest philosophical traditions in history. For centuries the thinkers of Islam wrestled with the deepest questions a mind can ask — whether the world was eternal or created, whether reason or revelation is the surer guide, whether the soul survives the body. Their answers shaped not only Islam but, carried into Europe, the whole later course of Western thought.

maps.title: From Baghdad to Córdoba
maps.caption: Islamic philosophy flowered at the two ends of the Muslim world — in the eastern lands of al-Farabi and Ibn Sina, and in the Spanish west of Ibn Rushd, whose work would reshape Christian Europe.

images.title: Ibn Sina (Avicenna)
images.caption: The Persian philosopher-physician whose grand synthesis of Aristotle and Islam shaped thought from Bukhara to Paris for six hundred years.

images.title: Ibn Rushd (Averroes)
images.caption: The philosopher of Córdoba whose commentaries on Aristotle were so authoritative that medieval Europe knew him simply as "the Commentator."

videos.title: Reason and Revelation in Islam
videos.caption: How Muslim thinkers reconciled the philosophy of Greece with the faith of the Quran — and changed the mind of the medieval world.

citations.label: Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy — Arabic and Islamic Philosophy

body.text: When Athens Met Mecca

The philosophy of Islam was born of an encounter. As the translators of the [Islamic Golden Age](/article/islamic-golden-age) rendered the works of Aristotle and Plato into Arabic, Muslim thinkers found themselves heirs to two great inheritances at once: the revelation of the [Quran](/article/the-quran), given by God, and the reasoning of [Greek philosophy](/article/greek-philosophy), won by the unaided human mind. The central drama of Islamic philosophy — its great question across the centuries — was how these two could be reconciled. Did reason and revelation lead to the same truth by different roads? And if they seemed to conflict, which should yield?

The earliest of the philosophers, known in Arabic as the **falasifa** (a word borrowed straight from the Greek *philosophia*), held that they could not truly conflict, since both came ultimately from God. The first great figure, **al-Kindi**, in ninth-century Baghdad, argued that the pursuit of truth through philosophy was a religious duty. After him **al-Farabi**, called "the Second Teacher" (Aristotle being the first), built a vast system uniting Greek logic and metaphysics with Islamic and Platonic ideals of the just society, imagining the ideal state as one led by a philosopher-prophet.

body.title: The eternal world problem
body.text: One question above all set philosophers and theologians at odds. Aristotle had argued that the universe was eternal, with no beginning in time. But the Quran teaches that God *created* the world, calling it into being out of nothing. Here Greek reason and Islamic revelation seemed flatly to contradict each other. How the philosophers handled this clash — whether they bent Aristotle to fit scripture, reinterpreted scripture, or held that philosophy and religion were two languages for one truth — became the great fault line of Islamic thought, and the spark for its fiercest debates.
Avicenna and the Grand Synthesis

The towering figure of the eastern tradition was **Ibn Sina**, known to the Latin world as **Avicenna** (980–1037). A prodigy who had mastered medicine and metaphysics in his youth, he built the most complete philosophical system the Muslim world had yet seen, fusing Aristotle with Neoplatonism and the faith of Islam. His proof of God as the "Necessary Existent" — the one being whose very essence is to exist, on which all other, merely possible things depend — became one of the most influential arguments in the history of philosophy, echoing through the work of Jewish and Christian thinkers for centuries.

Avicenna's reach went far beyond his own faith. His distinction between essence and existence, his analysis of the soul, his famous "floating man" thought experiment — imagining a person created in midair, with no sensation of the body, who would still know that they exist — entered the bloodstream of medieval European philosophy. Through him, and through the [Golden Age's](/article/islamic-golden-age) translations, Aristotle returned to a Europe that had largely lost him.
The world is divided into men who have wit and no religion, and men who have religion and no wit.

That sharp saying, attributed to the eleventh-century poet-philosopher al-Maʿarri, captures the tension the falasifa lived with — the suspicion, on both sides, between the party of reason and the party of faith.
Al-Ghazali and the Limits of Reason

The boldness of the philosophers provoked a brilliant reply. The theologian **al-Ghazali** (1058–1111), one of the most influential thinkers in all of Islam, mastered philosophy in order to critique it. In his *Incoherence of the Philosophers* he argued that reason, for all its power, overreached when it claimed to prove things like the eternity of the world or to dictate the nature of God — matters that belong to revelation. He went further, questioning whether the chain of cause and effect the philosophers trusted was a necessity at all, or simply the habitual way God chooses to act.

Al-Ghazali did not reject reason; he was himself a master of logic. But he set limits to it, and after a personal crisis he turned toward the inner, experiential path of **Sufism**, the mystical tradition that sought not to prove God but to *know* Him directly, in the heart. In this turn he stands close to the contemplatives of other faiths — to the [Christian mystics](/article/christian-mysticism) who sought union with God beyond argument, and to the sages of [India](/article/hindu-philosophy) for whom the highest truth was realised in experience rather than reasoned to. His enormous prestige reshaped the balance of Islamic thought for centuries after.
Averroes and the Gift to Europe

In the far west, in Muslim Spain, the tradition produced its last great defender of philosophy: **Ibn Rushd** of Córdoba, known to Europe as **Averroes** (1126–1198). Stung by al-Ghazali's attack, he wrote a point-by-point reply, *The Incoherence of the Incoherence*, defending reason and insisting that philosophy and religion were two paths to the same truth — one for the learned, one for the many — that could never genuinely contradict. His vast commentaries on Aristotle were so penetrating that the philosophers of Christian Europe knew him simply as "the Commentator."

body.title: The road back to Europe
body.text: History holds a striking irony. Just as Averroes was defending philosophy in the Muslim west, the centre of gravity of Islamic thought was shifting toward al-Ghazali's more cautious vision. But Averroes's works, translated from Arabic into Latin, exploded into the universities of Christian Europe, where they forced thinkers like Thomas Aquinas to confront Aristotle head-on and helped set off the great scholastic revival. Islamic philosophy thus did for Europe what it had done for the ancient world before — it preserved, sharpened and handed on the tools of reason, shaping a civilization beyond its own.
A Living Inheritance

The classical age of the falasifa eventually waned, but Islamic philosophy never died. In Persia a rich tradition of mystical philosophy continued for centuries, culminating in thinkers like Mulla Sadra, who wove together reason, revelation and inner vision into a grand metaphysics of being. And the questions the tradition raised — the relation of faith and reason, the nature of the soul, the proof of God, the reach and the limits of the human mind — remain as alive today as when Aristotle's words first echoed in the libraries of Baghdad. In wrestling so deeply with them, the philosophers of Islam not only enriched their own faith but helped shape the mind of the world.

title: Jesus of Nazareth
eyebrow: The carpenter who changed the world
lede: No single life has been pondered more deeply than that of Jesus of Nazareth — a Jewish teacher who taught for perhaps three years, wrote nothing, led no army, and was executed in his thirties, yet whose memory split history in two. To his followers he is the Son of God risen from the dead; to historians he is one of the best-attested figures of the ancient world whose inner reality remains forever beyond proof. Faith and history meet here, and where they part this article does not pretend to judge.

maps.title: The World of Galilee and Judea
maps.caption: From Nazareth in the Galilean hills to the Sea of Galilee and the temple city of Jerusalem — the small stage on which the whole story unfolds, all within a Roman province.

images.title: The Sea of Galilee
images.caption: The freshwater lake whose fishing villages — Capernaum, Bethsaida — were the setting for most of Jesus's recorded teaching and his first followers.

images.title: The Crucifixion
images.caption: The death by which the early Christians believed the world was redeemed — a Roman punishment turned, in faith, into the hinge of history.

videos.title: The Historical Jesus
videos.caption: What scholars can and cannot say about the man behind the gospels, weighed without judgement.

citations.label: Bart D. Ehrman — Did Jesus Exist?

body.text: Why This Article Is Marked "Theory"

Almost no serious historian doubts that a Jewish man named Jesus lived in early first-century Galilee, gathered followers, and was crucified under the Roman prefect Pontius Pilate. The Roman writers Tacitus and the Jewish historian Josephus both mention him within a lifetime or two of his death, and the letters of Paul were written barely twenty years after the crucifixion. That much is the bedrock of mainstream scholarship.

What lies beyond proof is everything that matters most to faith. Whether Jesus was the Son of God, whether he worked miracles, whether he rose bodily from the tomb — these are claims of a different order, held by believers as the deepest truths there are and set aside by historians as beyond the reach of their tools. This article marks itself **theory** not to cast doubt on Christian conviction but to be honest about where the historian's road ends and the pilgrim's begins.

body.title: History and faith
body.text: The gospels are not neutral biographies; they are testimonies of faith, written to proclaim Jesus as Lord. That does not make them worthless to the historian — it makes them passionate, early and specific. But it does mean that reading them is always an act of interpretation. We present here both what scholarship reconstructs and what the church confesses, and we let the reader hold the two together.
A Life in Galilee

Jesus was born around **4 BCE**, in the last years of Herod the Great, into a world ruled at a distance by [Rome](/article/roman-empire) and close at hand by its client kings. He grew up in Nazareth, an unremarkable Galilean village, in a family of modest craftsmen — the Greek word used of him and his father suggests a builder or carpenter. For most of his life he is invisible to history.

Then, perhaps around the year 28, he appeared as a wandering teacher. He proclaimed the coming of the **Kingdom of God**, healed the sick, ate with outcasts and tax collectors, and taught in vivid parables drawn from the fields and households of ordinary people. He gathered an inner circle of twelve and a wider crowd of followers, women prominent among them. His message was urgent and unsettling: that God's reign was breaking in, that the last would be first, and that love of God and neighbour fulfilled the whole law.
Blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are those who mourn, for they shall be comforted. Blessed are the meek, for they shall inherit the earth.

The Sermon on the Mount, from which those lines come, remains one of the most influential texts ever spoken — its ethic of mercy, forgiveness and nonviolence has stirred reformers and revolutionaries far beyond the church.
The Road to Jerusalem

The gospels converge on a final journey to **Jerusalem**, the holy city, at Passover. There Jesus caused an uproar in the Temple, was welcomed by crowds, and within days was betrayed, arrested, and handed to the Roman authorities. He was condemned and crucified — a punishment Rome reserved for those it deemed rebels — beneath a placard mocking him as "King of the Jews." He died, by the traditional reckoning, around the year **30**.

For his followers this was not the end but the turning point. They proclaimed that on the third day the tomb was found empty and that he appeared to them alive. Whatever a historian makes of those reports, something happened that transformed a scattered, frightened band into a movement willing to die for its message. That transformation is the seed from which [the early church](/article/early-church) and all of [Christianity](/article/christianity) grew.

body.title: A Jewish life
body.text: It is easy to forget that Jesus lived and died a Jew, keeping the Sabbath and the festivals, steeped in the Hebrew scriptures and the prophets. He did not set out to found a new religion; he spoke to Israel, in the idiom of Israel. The faith that grew up in his name — drawing in Greeks and Romans and reaching far beyond the world of the synagogue and the gods of [Rome](/article/roman-religion) — would have astonished his first hearers as much as anyone.
The Question That Will Not Rest

Two thousand years on, the figure of Jesus refuses to settle into the past. Philosophers and poets, sceptics and saints have measured themselves against him. Some see a sublime moral teacher, some a prophet of the coming kingdom, some the very Word of God made flesh. The gospels themselves record his own question to his friends — *"Who do you say that I am?"* — and leave it ringing down the centuries. Every age has answered it again. That this unanswerable question still grips so much of humankind is, perhaps, the surest historical fact of all.

toc.text: The Question That Will Not Rest

title: Julius Caesar
eyebrow: The man who broke the Republic
lede: Soldier, statesman, and writer of genius, Julius Caesar conquered Gaul, crossed the Rubicon into civil war, loved a queen of Egypt, and made himself master of Rome — only to fall beneath the daggers of senators who killed him to save a Republic that died with him anyway.

maps.title: The Conquest of Gaul
maps.caption: Caesar's eight-year campaign (58–50 BCE) brought all of modern France and Belgium under Roman rule and made him the richest man in the state.

maps.title: The Civil War
maps.caption: From the Rubicon to Pharsalus to Egypt — the campaigns by which Caesar defeated Pompey and the Senate.

images.title: The Tusculum portrait
images.caption: One of the few likenesses of Caesar carved within living memory of the man himself.

images.title: Caesar and Cleopatra
images.caption: The Roman general and the last pharaoh of Egypt — a political alliance that became a legend.

videos.title: Crossing the Rubicon
videos.caption: The day a general led his army against his own city and changed the course of Roman history.

citations.label: Julius Caesar — Commentarii de Bello Gallico
citations.source: Caesar's own account of the Gallic War (Loeb Classical Library)

citations.label: Suetonius — The Life of the Deified Julius
citations.source: Suetonius, The Twelve Caesars (Loeb Classical Library)

body.text: A Patrician on the Rise

Gaius Julius Caesar was born in **100 BCE** into an old but faded patrician family that claimed descent from the goddess **Venus** herself — a pedigree he would later turn to political advantage. He came of age in the violent, factional Rome of [the late Republic](/article/roman-republic), where the murders of Marius and Sulla had shown that armies, not arguments, now decided who ruled. A gifted speaker and a shameless borrower of money, Caesar climbed the ladder of offices by charm, bribery, and an unfailing instinct for where power was moving.

His decisive move was an alliance. In **60 BCE** he joined with Rome's richest man, **Crassus**, and its greatest general, **Pompey**, in the informal pact historians call the First Triumvirate. With their backing he won the consulship — and then the prize that would make him: a long military command in Gaul.
The Conquest of Gaul

For eight years Caesar waged war across the lands of modern France and Belgium, and even raided Britain and crossed the Rhine into Germany. By the campaign's end he had defeated hundreds of tribes, killed or enslaved enormous numbers, and added a vast new province to the empire. He recorded it all himself in the *Commentaries on the Gallic War*, a masterpiece of clear Latin prose written, tellingly, in the third person — propaganda so well crafted that it is still read as literature two thousand years later.

body.title: Caesar wrote his own legend
body.text: By publishing dispatches in plain, vivid Latin, Caesar made himself the hero of his own story in the minds of ordinary Romans. He understood, as few generals have, that the account of a victory can matter as much as the victory itself. Gaul made Caesar fabulously rich and gave him a battle-hardened [army](/article/roman-army) devoted to him personally. To the frightened Senate, that combination looked exactly like the tyranny the Republic had been built to prevent. ## Crossing the Rubicon When the Senate, prodded by Pompey, ordered Caesar to disband his army and return to Rome as a private citizen — where his enemies waited to destroy him — he chose war instead. In **January 49 BCE** he led a single legion across the **Rubicon**, the small river marking the boundary a general was forbidden to cross under arms. > The die is cast. With those words, tradition says, he plunged Rome into civil war. The campaign that followed carried him the length of the Mediterranean. He broke Pompey's larger army at **Pharsalus** in Greece, and when Pompey fled to [Egypt](/article/ancient-egypt), Caesar pursued. ## Egypt and Cleopatra In Alexandria, Caesar was drawn into a dynastic quarrel of the Ptolemies and into the arms of **Cleopatra VII**, the brilliant young queen who would become the last [pharaoh](/article/the-pharaoh) of Egypt. He restored her to a throne she shared, fathered a son she named Caesarion, and lingered in the richest kingdom of the ancient world. The meeting of the Roman master of the west and the Greek queen of the Nile became one of history's enduring legends — and bound Rome's fate ever closer to Egypt's grain and gold. ## The Ides of March Returning to Rome supreme, Caesar gathered every honour into himself: **dictator for life**, his image on the coinage, a month renamed *July* in his honour. He reformed the calendar, relieved debt, and planned grand works — but he also wore the trappings of a king in a city that still swore its old oath against them. To a band of senators led by **Brutus** and **Cassius**, this was tyranny that had to be cut out. On **15 March 44 BCE** — the Ides of March — they surrounded him in the Senate and stabbed him twenty-three times at the foot of Pompey's statue. :::note The murder that backfired The assassins killed Caesar to save the Republic, but they had no plan for what came next. Their act unleashed another round of civil war, at the end of which Caesar's heir Octavian — soon to be **Augustus** — gathered all power into one pair of hands and founded the [Roman Empire](/article/roman-empire). In death Caesar achieved what the conspirators most feared: he became a god, and his name became the very word for emperor — *Kaiser*, *Tsar*, *Caesar*.

title: Mars
lede: Half the size of Earth, painted rust-red by iron in its soil, and wrapped in a thin cold air, Mars is the world that has gripped human imagination like no other. Once warm and wet, today a frozen desert, it is the most explored planet beyond our own — and the leading candidate for humanity's first steps onto another world.

maps.title: The face of Mars
maps.caption: Olympus Mons, the vast Tharsis volcanoes, the Valles Marineris canyon system, and the polar ice caps.

images.title: The rusty surface
images.caption: A cold, dry, iron-stained desert under a pink-tinged sky — photographed from the ground by NASA's rovers.

images.title: Ancient riverbeds
images.caption: Branching channels and layered sediments — evidence that liquid water once flowed across the Martian surface.
images.credit: NASA / JPL / University of Arizona, HiRISE

images.title: Ingenuity in flight
images.caption: The first powered, controlled flight on another planet — a small helicopter that flew in Mars's thin air in 2021.

videos.title: A world that lost its water
videos.caption: How Mars went from a warmer, wetter past to the frozen desert we see today.

videos.title: Driving on Mars
videos.caption: How robotic rovers explore the surface, drill rock, and search for signs of ancient life.

body.text: The Red Planet

Mars glows a distinctive rusty red in the night sky, and the reason is literally rust: its surface is rich in **iron oxide**, the same compound that reddens corroded metal on Earth. The ancients named it after gods of war for that blood-coloured light — the Romans called it Mars, and we still do.

It is a smaller, colder, drier sibling of Earth. Mars is about half our planet's diameter and has roughly a third of its gravity, so a person who weighs 60 kilograms on Earth would weigh barely 23 on Mars. Its thin atmosphere is almost entirely carbon dioxide and exerts less than one percent of Earth's surface pressure — far too little to breathe, and too thin to hold much warmth. Surface temperatures average a frigid −60°C.

body.title: A day that feels like home
body.text: Despite all its differences, Mars keeps a familiar rhythm. A Martian day — called a **sol** — lasts 24 hours and 39 minutes, remarkably close to Earth's. Its axis is tilted much like ours, too, so Mars has seasons. These similarities are part of why it feels, of all the planets, the most like a place humans could one day live.
A Landscape of Extremes

For a small world, Mars has the most dramatic terrain in the Solar System. It is home to **Olympus Mons**, the largest known volcano anywhere — a gently sloping mountain about 22 kilometres high, nearly three times the height of Everest, and so broad it would cover much of a country. Stretching across the equator is **Valles Marineris**, a system of canyons over 4,000 kilometres long and in places several kilometres deep, dwarfing the Grand Canyon.

At its poles lie shining **ice caps** of frozen water and carbon dioxide that grow and shrink with the seasons. Vast dust storms occasionally swell to engulf the entire planet, and fine red dust coats everything — a constant hazard for the robots, and one day the people, who explore it.
The Ghost of Water

Mars today is bone dry at the surface, but it was not always so. Orbiters and rovers have found unmistakable signatures of a watery past: dried-up **river valleys**, deltas where rivers once emptied into lakes, rounded pebbles shaped by flowing water, and minerals that only form in its presence. Billions of years ago, Mars appears to have been warmer and wetter, with rivers, lakes, perhaps even a shallow ocean.

Where there was water, there may have been life. The search for evidence of ancient Martian microbes — long extinct or perhaps still clinging on underground — is the single greatest driver of Mars exploration.

So what happened? Mars lost its protective magnetic field early in its history, and without that shield the solar wind gradually stripped away most of its atmosphere. As the air thinned, the planet grew cold, its water froze or escaped to space, and the world we now see was left behind. Much of its remaining water survives as ice, locked in the poles and buried beneath the surface.
Two Little Moons

Mars has two small, lumpy moons, **Phobos** and **Deimos** — names meaning "fear" and "dread," the companions of the war god. They are most likely captured asteroids, just a few kilometres across. Phobos orbits so close and so fast that it crosses the Martian sky twice a day and is slowly spiralling inward; in tens of millions of years it will break apart, perhaps giving Mars a temporary ring.
A Planet Full of Robots
No world beyond Earth has been studied as intensely as Mars. A fleet of orbiters maps it from above, while a lineage of **rovers** has trundled across its surface, growing ever more capable:

items: **Sojourner** (1997) — the first wheels on Mars, a microwave-sized proof of concept.
**Spirit and Opportunity** (2004) — twin geologists that found firm evidence of past water; Opportunity worked for nearly 15 years.
**Curiosity** (2012) — a car-sized nuclear-powered laboratory that confirmed Mars once had habitable conditions.
**Perseverance** (2021) — now caching rock samples for a future return to Earth, and carrying **Ingenuity**, the first aircraft to fly on another planet.

body.text: These missions, and the engineering feats behind landing on a distant world, are explored further in [space missions](/article/space-missions).
The Next Giant Leap

Of all the planets, Mars is the one humans seriously plan to visit. It is reachable, it has water ice and raw materials, and its day-length and seasons make it comparatively hospitable — by the brutal standards of space. Both national space agencies and private companies are designing the rockets, habitats, and life-support systems that a crewed mission would require.

The obstacles are immense: a journey of six to nine months each way, exposure to space radiation, the challenge of living off the land, and the sheer cost. But Mars represents the most concrete answer to an old question — whether humanity will become a species that lives on more than one world. That ambition, and what a future on the red planet might look like, runs through [the future of space](/article/the-future-of-space).

title: Modern Egypt
eyebrow: From the pharaohs to the present
lede: The land of the pharaohs is also a vibrant modern nation of more than a hundred million people — shaped by Islam and Arabic, by the Suez Canal and the Aswan High Dam, and by an unbroken sense of one of the world's oldest identities.

maps.title: The Arab Republic of Egypt
maps.caption: Cairo, Alexandria, the Nile valley and delta, the Suez Canal and the Sinai Peninsula.

images.title: Cairo on the Nile
images.caption: The largest city in the Arab world, where medieval minarets meet a modern megacity.

images.title: The Suez Canal
images.caption: Opened 1869, the artificial waterway linking the Mediterranean and the Red Sea.

videos.title: A nation on one river
videos.caption: How 100 million people still live clustered along the same green Nile valley.

citations.label: The British Museum — Egypt after the pharaohs

body.text: The Long Aftermath

When Cleopatra VII died in 30 BCE, Egypt became a province of **Rome**, then of **Byzantium**. In 641 CE the **Arab conquest** brought Islam and the Arabic language, which gradually became the country's faith and tongue. Over the centuries Egypt was ruled from afar and up close — by the Fatimids and Mamluks, by the Ottomans, and after Napoleon's brief expedition, increasingly under European influence.
Building the Modern Nation
Two great engineering projects reshaped the country:

items: The **Suez Canal** (opened 1869) cut a sea route between the Mediterranean and the Red Sea, making Egypt a strategic crossroads of world trade — and a target of imperial rivalry.

The **Aswan High Dam** (completed 1970) finally tamed the annual Nile flood, generating power and securing water, but ending the natural cycle of silt that had fed Egyptian farming for five thousand years.

body.text: Egypt won full independence over the first half of the twentieth century, becoming a **republic in 1953**. Today it is the most populous Arab country and a cultural powerhouse of film, music, and scholarship.

body.title: An identity that never broke
body.text: Few modern nations sit so directly on top of their ancient selves. Egyptians today live on the same river, beneath the same monuments, carrying a continuous sense of "Egypt" that stretches back five millennia.
The Past as a Living Resource

The legacy of [the pharaohs](/article/the-pharaoh) is not only a matter of pride; it is a pillar of the economy. The monuments of [ancient Egypt](/article/ancient-egypt) draw millions of visitors each year, making heritage one of the nation's great modern industries — the story we follow next, into [Egyptian tourism](/article/egypt-tourism).

toc.text: The Past as a Living Resource

title: Muhammad
lede: Muhammad ibn Abdullah was a merchant of Mecca who, at the age of forty, began to receive what he proclaimed as revelations from God — and in the twenty-three years that followed transformed the fractious tribes of Arabia into a single community and founded a faith now followed by nearly two billion people. Better documented than almost any figure of the ancient world, he is to Muslims not a divine being but the last and greatest of God's prophets, the human channel through whom the final revelation came. Few lives have so completely redirected the course of history.

maps.title: The World of Mecca and Medina
maps.caption: The trading towns of the Hijaz, along the western coast of Arabia — from Mecca, where Muhammad was born, to Medina, where he built the first Muslim community some 320 kilometres to the north.

images.title: The Cave of Hira
images.caption: The cave on the mountain above Mecca where, tradition holds, Muhammad received the first revelation — the command "Read!" from the angel Gabriel.

images.title: The Prophet's Mosque at Medina
images.caption: Built around Muhammad's own house, the mosque in Medina holds his tomb and is the second-holiest site in Islam, its green dome known to Muslims everywhere.

videos.title: The Life of the Prophet
videos.caption: From orphan and merchant to prophet and statesman — the life that founded a world religion in a single generation.

citations.label: Ibn Ishaq — The Life of Muhammad

body.text: An Orphan of Mecca

Muhammad was born around the year 570 in **Mecca**, a dusty trading town in the barren hills of western Arabia that was also a centre of pilgrimage, home to the ancient cube-shaped shrine called the **Kaaba**, then crowded with the idols of the Arabian tribes. His early life was marked by loss. His father died before his birth, his mother when he was six, and he was raised first by his grandfather and then by his uncle Abu Talib. He grew into a trusted merchant, known by the nickname **al-Amin**, "the trustworthy," and married a older widow and businesswoman named **Khadija**, who would become his first and most devoted supporter.

Arabia in his day stood at the edge of two great empires — the Christian Eastern Roman world to the north-west and the Zoroastrian Persian empire to the north-east — yet its own tribes worshipped many gods and were bound by no single faith or state. It was a society of fierce clan loyalty, blood feud and trade, where the weak had little protection. Into this world Muhammad would bring a message of one God and one community that cut across every tribal line.

body.title: The night of power
body.text: Tradition holds that around the year 610, when Muhammad was about forty, he had withdrawn to meditate in a cave on Mount Hira above Mecca when the angel Gabriel appeared and commanded him: **"Recite!"** Overwhelmed and terrified, he protested that he could not — and the words of the first revelation poured through him. Muslims call this the *Laylat al-Qadr*, the Night of Power, and reckon it the moment the [Quran](/article/the-quran) began to descend. For the rest of his life the revelations continued, and he proclaimed them not as his own words but as the speech of God.
The Preacher and His Persecution

Muhammad began to preach in Mecca a message of startling simplicity and danger: that there was only one God, that the idols of the Kaaba were nothing, that the rich owed justice to the poor and the orphan, and that all would be raised to face judgement. To the merchant aristocracy of Mecca, whose wealth and prestige flowed from the pilgrimage to those very idols, this was both blasphemy and a threat to their livelihood. His small band of followers — slaves, the young, the poor and a few of the well-born — were mocked, boycotted and persecuted.

His message had deep kinship with the faiths already in the region. Like the prophets of the [Hebrew Bible](/article/the-bible) and like [Jesus of Nazareth](/article/jesus-of-nazareth), Muhammad called people from idols to the one God of Abraham, warned of a coming judgement, and demanded mercy and justice. Muslims revere Jesus as a mighty prophet and the Messiah, born of the Virgin Mary, though they do not hold him to be divine. The God Muhammad preached was, in his teaching, the same God — the message restored to its original purity.
Recite in the name of your Lord who created — created man from a clinging form. Recite! Your Lord is the Most Bountiful One, who taught by the pen, taught man what he did not know.
The Hijra and the Birth of a Community

By 622 the pressure in Mecca had become unbearable, and Muhammad accepted an invitation from the town of **Yathrib**, to the north, to come and govern it. This migration — the **Hijra** — is the hinge of Islamic history, so decisive that Muslims count their calendar from that year, not from the Prophet's birth. Yathrib was renamed **Medina**, "the city," and there Muhammad became not only a prophet but a lawgiver, judge and head of state, binding the quarrelling clans and the growing body of believers into a single community, the **umma**.

The years that followed brought open war with Mecca, a series of hard-fought battles, and at last, in 630, the bloodless conquest of the city itself. Muhammad entered Mecca in triumph, cleansed the Kaaba of its idols, and rededicated it to the one God — restoring, as Muslims believe, the shrine that Abraham himself had first raised. The faith he had been mocked for now spread swiftly across the peninsula.

body.title: Prophet, not divine
body.text: It is a point Muslims guard with great care: Muhammad is not worshipped, and is never held to be divine. He is the *seal of the prophets* — the final and greatest of a line stretching back to Adam — but he is a man, mortal and fallible in worldly matters, chosen to carry God's word. Where Christians came to confess Jesus as God incarnate, Islam draws an absolute line between the creator and every creature, however exalted. To honour Muhammad is one thing; to worship him would be the very idolatry he came to abolish.
A Life That Reshaped the World

Muhammad died in Medina in 632, only two years after the conquest of Mecca, leaving no clear successor — a silence that would soon open the great [Sunni–Shia divide](/article/sunni-shia-divide). He left behind no empire of stone, but something far more durable: a book held to be the words of God, a community that would within a century rule from Spain to India, and a model of life imitated in its smallest details by a quarter of humanity. He had united a peninsula, founded a religion and a civilization, and set in motion a transformation of the world. Unlike most founders of faiths, he did all this within the full light of recorded history, his life and sayings gathered, debated and preserved by those who knew him — which is why Muslims can follow not only what he taught but how he lived.
