body.text: Horus on the Throne

The word *pharaoh* comes from **per-aa**, "the great house" — originally the palace itself, later its royal occupant. The king was the indispensable link between humanity and the gods. While he lived he was the earthly embodiment of **Horus**; when he died he became **Osiris**, and his successor took up the falcon's role. Kingship was therefore not just political office but a cosmic function: through the pharaoh, maat was maintained and the very order of the world renewed.
Crowns, Names, and Titles
Royal identity was written in regalia and language:

items: The **double crown** (*Pschent*) united the White Crown of Upper Egypt and the Red Crown of Lower Egypt — a wearable claim to rule both lands.
The **crook and flail** signalled the king as shepherd and provider.
The **false beard** and **uraeus** (the rearing cobra) marked divine authority.
Each king bore a **fivefold titulary** of throne-names and birth-names, framed in protective oval **cartouches**.

body.title: Pharaohs were not all men
body.text: The throne was overwhelmingly male, but several women ruled in their own right — most notably **Hatshepsut**, one of Egypt's most successful and prolific builders, who had herself depicted with the traditional regalia of kingship.
The Machinery of Rule

A god-king still needed a government. Beneath the pharaoh stood the **vizier**, the chief administrator, and an army of literate **scribes** who ran the granaries, the tax rolls, the courts, and the great building projects. Because wealth was measured in grain and stored centrally, the state reached into every village. This made Egypt one of history's first highly organised bureaucratic states — and explains how it could marshal the labour to raise the [Pyramids of Giza](/article/pyramids-of-giza).
The greatest builders — Khufu, Hatshepsut, Ramesses II — measured their reigns in stone, leaving monuments designed to proclaim a single, eternal message: *the king endures.*
That ambition to endure echoes across millennia into the symbols of [modern Egypt](/article/modern-egypt), where the pharaohs remain a source of national identity and pride.

title: The Planets
lede: From scorched Mercury to frigid Neptune, the eight planets of the Solar System are a gallery of wildly different worlds — rocky and gaseous, cratered and storm-wracked, ringed and barren. Together they reveal how varied a single star's family of planets can be.

maps.title: The eight planets to scale
maps.caption: The four small terrestrial worlds and the four giants, in order of distance from the Sun.

images.title: Earth, the blue marble
images.caption: The only known world with liquid water on its surface and life — so far, the one planet we know to be inhabited.

images.title: Jupiter and the Great Red Spot
images.caption: The largest planet, a banded gas giant whose centuries-old storm is wider than the Earth.

images.title: Saturn's rings
images.caption: A dazzling system of countless icy particles, only a few tens of metres thick yet tens of thousands of kilometres wide.

videos.title: A tour of the eight planets
videos.caption: A flythrough from Mercury to Neptune, comparing size, climate, and character.

videos.title: How we weigh and measure a planet
videos.caption: Orbits, transits, and the tricks astronomers use to map worlds they cannot visit.

body.text: What Makes a Planet

A planet, as the International Astronomical Union defines it, is a body that orbits the Sun, is massive enough for its own gravity to pull it into a round shape, and has cleared its orbital path of other debris. By that measure the Solar System has exactly **eight** planets. They fall into two clear families, a divide inherited from how the [Solar System](/article/the-solar-system) formed.

The four **terrestrial planets** — Mercury, Venus, Earth, and Mars — are small, dense, and rocky, with solid surfaces you could stand on. The four **giant planets** — Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune — are vast balls of gas and ice with no real surface at all, surrounded by rings and swarms of moons.

body.title: The order, and a way to remember it
body.text: Outward from the Sun: **Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune**. A classic mnemonic is "My Very Educated Mother Just Served Us Nachos." Note that the largest planets sit farthest out — a consequence of ice being abundant only in the cold outer Solar System.
The Rocky Inner Worlds

**Mercury**, closest to the Sun, is the smallest planet — a cratered, airless ball not much larger than the Moon. With almost no atmosphere to hold heat, it swings from scorching day to freezing night more violently than any other planet.

**Venus** is Earth's near-twin in size, but a hellish one. A thick atmosphere of carbon dioxide traps heat in a runaway **greenhouse effect**, making its surface hotter than Mercury's — around 465°C, hot enough to melt lead — beneath clouds of sulphuric acid. It is a sobering example of how a climate can go catastrophically wrong.

**Earth** is, so far, unique: the only known world with liquid water on its surface, a protective magnetic field, a breathable atmosphere, and life. Its single large [Moon](/article/the-moon) stabilises its tilt and drives the tides.

**Mars**, the rusty red desert world, is the most Earth-like of our neighbours and the great focus of exploration — a planet that once had rivers and lakes, and the most likely place to search for past life. Its story is told in full in [Mars](/article/mars).
The Giants of the Outer System
Beyond the asteroid belt, the planets become enormous.

items: **Jupiter** is the king — more than twice as massive as all the other planets combined. This banded gas giant is wrapped in storms, the greatest of which, the **Great Red Spot**, has raged for centuries and is wider than the Earth. Its powerful gravity helps shield the inner planets from stray comets.
**Saturn** is famous for its breathtaking **rings**, a glittering system of countless ice and rock particles. For all its size, Saturn is so light for its volume that it would float in water.
**Uranus** is an ice giant tipped onto its side, rolling around the Sun at an extreme tilt that gives it decades-long seasons. Its pale blue-green colour comes from methane in its atmosphere.
**Neptune**, the most distant planet, is a deep blue world of supersonic winds — the fastest in the Solar System — so far from the Sun that one of its years lasts 165 of ours.

body.text: Worlds Within Worlds

The giant planets are each surrounded by their own miniature systems of moons, some of which are among the most intriguing places in the Solar System. Jupiter's **Europa** and Saturn's **Enceladus** appear to hide liquid-water oceans beneath icy crusts; Saturn's **Titan**, larger than the planet Mercury, has a dense atmosphere and seas of liquid methane. If life exists elsewhere in the Solar System, these ocean moons are among the leading places to look.

body.title: Where did Pluto go?
body.text: For 76 years Pluto was counted as the ninth planet. In 2006 it was reclassified as a **dwarf planet**, because it has not cleared its orbit — it is one of many icy bodies in the Kuiper Belt. The change reflected a deeper discovery: that the Solar System holds whole populations of small worlds Pluto belongs to.
Planets Beyond the Sun

For most of history, the eight planets were the only ones we knew. That changed in **1995**, when astronomers detected the first planet orbiting another Sun-like star — a discovery later honoured with a Nobel Prize. These worlds around other stars are called **exoplanets**, and we have now confirmed thousands of them, with billions more thought to exist across the Milky Way.

The variety is staggering: scorching "hot Jupiters" that hug their stars, vast "super-Earths" with no analogue at home, and rocky worlds orbiting in the **habitable zone**, where temperatures might allow liquid water. Each discovery widens the question that the planets first raise: how common are worlds like Earth, and might any of them be home to life?
A Gallery of Possibilities

Taken together, the planets are a natural laboratory. Venus shows what a runaway greenhouse can do; Mars shows a world that lost its water and air; Jupiter shows how a giant can shepherd and protect its neighbours; the ocean moons hint that habitable conditions may be far more widespread than once believed.

Studying our own eight worlds is what lets us read the billions being found elsewhere — and it shapes our ambitions for the journey ahead, from robotic explorers to human footprints on other worlds, the subject of [space missions](/article/space-missions) and [the future of space](/article/the-future-of-space).

toc.text: The Giants of the Outer System

title: The Pyramids of Giza
lede: Three colossal tombs raised on a desert plateau over forty-five centuries ago — the most precise, most enduring, and only surviving wonder of the ancient world, and a monument to the organisational genius of the Old Kingdom.

maps.title: The Giza plateau
maps.caption: The pyramids of Khufu, Khafre and Menkaure with the Great Sphinx, southwest of modern Cairo.

images.title: The Great Pyramid of Khufu
images.caption: Originally 146.6 m tall and clad in polished white limestone that would have blazed in the sun.

images.title: The Great Sphinx
images.caption: A limestone lion with a human face, almost certainly bearing the likeness of Khafre.

images.title: Interior — the Grand Gallery
images.caption: A soaring corbel-vaulted passage rising to the King's Chamber inside Khufu's pyramid.

videos.title: Building the Great Pyramid
videos.caption: How ramps, levers, and a vast organised workforce raised 2.3 million blocks.

body.text: A Wonder That Outlasted the Rest

Of the **Seven Wonders of the Ancient World**, only one still stands: the **Great Pyramid of Khufu** at Giza. Completed around 2560 BCE, it held the record as the tallest structure made by human hands for nearly **3,800 years**, until the cathedral spires of medieval Europe finally surpassed it. Beside it rise the pyramids of **Khafre** (which looks taller because it sits on higher ground and still keeps a cap of its original casing) and the smaller **Menkaure**, guarded by the **Great Sphinx**.
Precision Without Iron

What stuns engineers is not just the size but the **accuracy**. The Great Pyramid's square base, some 230 metres on a side, is level to within a couple of centimetres, and its faces are aligned to true north with an error of only a fraction of a degree — achieved without iron tools, the compass, or modern surveying instruments.

items: An estimated **2.3 million blocks**, averaging over two tonnes each.
Original height **146.6 m**; a smooth casing of fine white limestone, now largely stripped away.
Internal chambers, relieving stones, and the soaring **Grand Gallery**, all cut and fitted with extraordinary care.

body.title: Not built by slaves
body.text: The old image of whip-driven slaves is a myth. Archaeology at the workers' village shows a paid, fed, and housed workforce of skilled craftsmen and rotating labourers — organised, provisioned, and even given medical care by the Egyptian state.
Tombs Aligned to the Sky

The pyramids were **royal tombs**, designed to launch the dead king into the heavens and eternal life. Their orientation was no accident: the Egyptians used the stars to fix true north, tying their architecture to the rhythms of the sky — a connection explored in [Egyptian astronomy](/article/egyptian-astronomy). Causeways linked each pyramid to a valley temple by the Nile, framing the whole complex as a machine for resurrection.
The shape itself may evoke the **benben** — the primeval mound that rose from the waters of creation, and the petrified rays of the sun god descending to the earth.

The pyramids are the supreme statement of a wider tradition of sacred building that runs through Egyptian temples and on into the story of [ancient architecture](/article/ancient-architecture). Today they remain the single most visited monument in the country — the gateway to [Egyptian tourism](/article/egypt-tourism).

toc.text: A Wonder That Outlasted the Rest

title: The Quran
lede: The Quran is, to Muslims, not a book written about God but the literal speech of God Himself — revealed to Muhammad over twenty-three years, recited and memorised before it was ever written, and preserved without alteration for fourteen centuries. Shorter than the New Testament yet recited in full from memory by millions, it is the most read, most memorised and most beautifully written book in the Arabic language, the foundation of a civilization and the living voice of the divine for nearly two billion believers.

maps.title: A Book Recited Across the World
maps.caption: Revealed in the Arabic of seventh-century Mecca and Medina, the Quran is recited in that same language from West Africa to Indonesia — its words unchanged by translation wherever Muslims pray.
maps.region: From Arabia to the wider Muslim world

images.title: An illuminated Quran
images.caption: For centuries the Quran has been copied by hand in exquisite calligraphy, its margins worked in gold — the supreme art of a faith that does not depict the divine in image.

images.title: A child learning to recite
images.caption: Across the Muslim world children memorise the entire Quran by heart, earning the honoured title of hafiz — keeper of the book — just as the first believers did.

videos.title: How the Quran Was Revealed
videos.caption: From spoken revelation and living memory to a single written text — the making of the book Muslims hold to be the words of God.

body.text: The Speech of God

The very name *Quran* means **recitation**, and that single word holds the key to what the book is. Muslims do not regard it as a record of religious experience or a collection of human testimony, as Christians read [the Bible](/article/the-bible). They hold it to be the direct, uncreated speech of God — the eternal word, existing with God before time, sent down in the Arabic tongue through the angel Gabriel to [Muhammad](/article/muhammad), who recited what he received. The Prophet is the channel; the words are God's own. This is why the Quran's authority in Islam is even more absolute than scripture in most other faiths: to a Muslim, to hear it recited is to hear God speak.

The revelations came not all at once but piecemeal, over the twenty-three years of Muhammad's prophetic life — sometimes a few verses, sometimes a whole passage, answering a question, meeting a crisis, consoling the persecuted or laying down a law. The earliest passages, given in Mecca, are short, fiery and lyrical, full of the wonder of creation and the terror of judgement. The later ones, from Medina, are longer and more concerned with how a community should live. Together they form a book of 114 chapters, called **suras**, arranged not in the order they were revealed nor by theme, but roughly from the longest to the shortest.

body.title: A miracle of language
body.text: Islam holds that every prophet was given a miracle suited to his age — Moses his staff, Jesus his healings — and that Muhammad's miracle was the Quran itself. Its claim to be divine rests above all on its language: a beauty, rhythm and power of Arabic that Muslims hold no human author could match. The Quran challenges any doubter to produce even a single chapter like it — and the tradition is that none ever has. This is the doctrine of *i'jaz*, the inimitability of the Quran, and it is why the book has never truly been "translated." Renderings into other languages are called *interpretations*; the Quran itself exists only in Arabic.
How a Recitation Became a Book

The Quran was a spoken thing before it was a written one. Muhammad's followers learned the revelations by heart as they came, and recited them in prayer; some wrote fragments on parchment, palm leaves, stone and bone. The faith placed extraordinary value on memorisation, and the *hafiz* — one who holds the entire Quran in memory — remains an honoured figure to this day. After the Prophet's death, as the generation who had heard him began to die in the wars of expansion, the community feared the text might fracture, and under the early caliphs an authoritative written version was gathered and standardised. That text has been transmitted, Muslims hold, unchanged ever since.

This careful guarding of an oral revelation invites comparison with the [Vedas](/article/vedas) of India, preserved across a thousand years by flawless recitation before they were ever committed to writing. Both traditions trusted the trained human memory above the fragile written page, and both treated the *sound* of the sacred words — not merely their meaning — as holy. To recite the Quran beautifully, with the rules of *tajwid* governing every vowel and pause, is itself an act of worship.
This is the Scripture in which there is no doubt, a guidance for those who are mindful of God.
That verse, near the very opening, announces the Quran's purpose: not poetry for its own sake, but guidance — a map for living rightly before God.
A Book of Guidance and Law

The Quran ranges across the whole of life. It tells the stories of the prophets — Adam and Noah, Abraham and Moses, and Jesus and Mary, who are honoured in its pages — drawing on the same sacred history as the [Bible](/article/the-bible) yet retelling it in its own voice. It paints vivid pictures of paradise and hell, proclaims the oneness and mercy of God, and lays down rules for prayer, charity, marriage, inheritance, trade and war. From its verses, together with the example of the Prophet, the scholars of Islam built the **sharia**, the comprehensive law of Muslim life.

body.title: Faith and reason
body.text: The Quran repeatedly calls on its readers to *think*, to observe the order of the heavens and the earth as signs of their maker. That summons helped fuel the great flowering of learning in the [Islamic Golden Age](/article/islamic-golden-age), when Muslim scholars, holding that the study of nature honoured its creator, eagerly absorbed the reasoning of [Greek philosophy](/article/greek-philosophy) and pushed it further. Where some traditions set faith against inquiry, the Quran's many verses urging reflection gave the early Muslim world a powerful warrant for the pursuit of knowledge.
The Living Heart of a Faith

No book is woven more deeply into the daily life of those who hold it sacred. The Quran is recited in the five daily prayers, chanted from minarets, inscribed in gold across the walls of mosques, studied in vast schools and whispered over the newborn and the dying. Its opening chapter, the *Fatiha*, is repeated by a Muslim more often than any other words in any language on Earth. Across fourteen centuries and a quarter of humanity, in lands that share neither tongue nor custom, the same Arabic verses are recited in the same way — a single book binding nearly two billion people to one another and, they believe, to the very voice of God.

title: The Ramayana
lede: The Ramayana is the most beloved story of the Hindu world — the tale of Prince Rama, his devoted wife Sita carried off by a demon king, and the war fought across the sea to win her back. For more than two thousand years it has set the pattern of the ideal life, and its hero, an avatar of Vishnu, remains one of the most worshipped figures on Earth.

maps.title: Rama's Long Road South
maps.caption: From the city of Ayodhya through the forests of exile to the southern straits and the bridge to Lanka — the geography of the epic is still mapped onto the Indian landscape.

images.title: Rama, Sita and Lakshmana
images.caption: The prince, his wife and his loyal brother in the years of forest exile — an image of devotion and dharma painted in every Indian style.

images.title: Hanuman leaps to Lanka
images.caption: The monkey-god crosses the ocean in a single bound to find the captive Sita — the supreme emblem of fearless devotion.

videos.title: The ideal and the exile
videos.caption: How a prince's banishment became the story a civilisation tells about duty, love and loyalty.

citations.label: The Ramayana of Valmiki
citations.source: The Ramayana of Valmiki (Princeton Library of Asian Translations)

citations.label: The Met — The Ramayana in Indian art

body.text: The First Poem

Tradition calls the Ramayana the **adi-kavya**, the "first poem" — the work in which the sage **Valmiki** is said to have invented the very art of verse, his grief at the death of a bird crystallising spontaneously into metre. Composed perhaps around 300 BCE and growing over the following centuries, it is shorter and more shapely than the sprawling [Mahabharata](/article/mahabharata): some twenty-four thousand verses telling a single, luminous story with the clean inevitability of a fable.

That story is quickly told. **Rama**, the noble prince of **Ayodhya**, is about to be crowned heir when a palace intrigue sends him into fourteen years of exile in the forest. His wife **Sita** and his devoted brother **Lakshmana** go with him, choosing hardship over separation. In the wilderness the demon king **Ravana**, lord of the island fortress of **Lanka**, abducts Sita and carries her across the sea. To rescue her, Rama allies with an army of monkeys, led by the mighty **Hanuman**, builds a bridge to the island, slays Ravana in a great war, and at last returns home to be crowned. The exile ends; light returns to Ayodhya; and the homecoming is remembered every year in the festival of lamps, **Diwali**.

body.title: A god who does not know he is God
body.text: Rama is an avatar of [Vishnu](/article/hindu-gods), the preserver descended to rid the world of a demon no god could otherwise destroy. Yet for most of the poem Rama lives and suffers as a man — grieving, doubting, weeping for his lost wife — apparently unaware of his own divinity. This is the heart of the Ramayana's appeal. It shows the divine choosing the full burden of human duty, and so makes the ideal life feel not remote but achievable.
The Pattern of the Ideal

More than any other Hindu text, the Ramayana is held up as a model for how to live. Each of its figures embodies a perfection of **dharma**. Rama is the ideal son, king and husband, who keeps his word though it costs him his throne and his happiness. Sita is the ideal of devotion and quiet courage, faithful through abduction and exile. Lakshmana is the perfect brother, Hanuman the perfect servant, and even the demon Ravana is a figure of tragic grandeur, a great scholar and king brought down by a single overmastering desire.

To call someone's reign **Ram Rajya**, "the rule of Rama," is still the highest praise an Indian can give a government: a golden age of justice, plenty and right order. For millennia parents have told the story to teach their children what loyalty, courage and faithfulness look like — a moral education delivered as an adventure.
Even the deathless gods envied the people of Ayodhya, who in the reign of Rama knew neither famine nor fear, neither sickness nor sorrow.
An Echo Across the Sea

The Ramayana invites comparison with another great ancient war fought across the water to recover a stolen wife — the Greek expedition against [Troy](/article/trojan-war). Both stories turn on an abduction, a long campaign overseas, and a fortress city brought down at last. But where the Greek epic broods on rage, pride and the futility of glory, the Indian one is finally a story of devotion and order restored — of a hero who fights not for honour but for duty, and a homecoming that heals the world rather than emptying it. The two poems are like mirror images of the heroic age, each revealing what its civilisation most prized.

body.title: A story that crossed an ocean
body.text: The Ramayana is not India's alone. Carried by traders and monks, it took root across Southeast Asia, where Rama's tale is danced, carved and performed to this day — as the *Ramakien* of Thailand, the *Reamker* of Cambodia, and the shadow-puppet epics of Java and Bali. Few stories on Earth have been loved by so many peoples for so long, or made so thoroughly their own.
The Living Epic

The Ramayana has never been merely read. It is sung in villages, staged each autumn in the open-air dramas of **Ramlila**, recited in Tulsidas's beloved Hindi retelling, and watched by hundreds of millions in television and film. When a serialised version aired in India, streets emptied as families gathered before their screens. Its hero is among the most worshipped deities in the world, his name a common greeting and a prayer for the dying. Together with the Mahabharata, the Ramayana shows how the deepest ideals of [Hinduism](/article/hinduism) — duty done with grace, love kept through suffering, order restored from chaos — reached the heart of a civilisation not as commandments but as a story too beautiful ever to forget.

title: The Reformation
lede: In 1517 an obscure German monk nailed a list of grievances to a church door, and the unified Christianity of the medieval West shattered. The Reformation split the church, remade Europe, and unleashed forces — the printed word, the right of private conscience, the vernacular Bible — whose consequences run straight through the modern world. It was at once a religious revolution and the birth pang of the age we still inhabit.

maps.title: A Continent Divided
maps.caption: Within a century the Reformation had split Europe — Protestant in much of the north, Catholic in the south — a religious map whose lines still shape the continent.

images.title: Luther's 95 Theses
images.caption: The document said to have been posted on the door of the castle church at Wittenberg in 1517 — the spark that lit the Reformation.

images.title: The printing press
images.caption: Gutenberg's invention turned a local dispute into a continental movement, spreading Luther's words faster than any authority could suppress them.

videos.title: The Reformation Explained
videos.caption: How one monk's protest broke Christendom and helped usher in the modern world.

body.text: A Door in Wittenberg

The traditional story has it that on the last day of October in **1517**, a professor of theology named **Martin Luther** posted ninety-five propositions on the door of the castle church in the German town of Wittenberg. He meant only to invite a scholarly debate about the sale of **indulgences** — papers that promised to shorten the buyer's time in purgatory, peddled to raise money for the rebuilding of St Peter's in Rome. He could not have imagined that he was lighting a fire that would consume the unity of Western [Christianity](/article/christianity).

Luther's protest struck a nerve because it touched a deep and growing unease. Many devout Christians had long been troubled by the wealth, corruption and worldliness of the late medieval church. What began as a quarrel about indulgences swiftly became a challenge to the whole structure of authority — to the power of the pope, the role of the priesthood, and the very means by which a soul is saved.

body.title: The heart of the matter
body.text: Luther's central conviction was deceptively simple: that human beings are reconciled to God not by good works, payments or priestly mediation, but by **faith alone**, as a free gift of grace. And the test of truth, he insisted, was not the authority of popes or councils but **scripture alone** — [the Bible](/article/the-bible), which every believer had the right and the duty to read. These two principles dynamited the foundations on which the medieval church had stood.
The Word Set Loose

What turned a German monk's protest into a European revolution was a machine. Gutenberg's printing press, barely sixty years old, seized on Luther's writings and scattered them across the continent faster than any authority could gather them up. His pamphlets sold in the tens of thousands; his translation of the Bible into vigorous, everyday German put scripture into the hands of ordinary people and helped forge the very language they spoke. To read the Bible for oneself, in one's own tongue, became a revolutionary act.

The movement spread and splintered. In Switzerland **Huldrych Zwingli** and then the formidable **John Calvin** built reformed churches of their own, Calvin's Geneva becoming a model for Protestants from Scotland to the Netherlands. In England the Reformation took a more political turn when **Henry VIII**, denied a divorce by Rome, broke with the pope and made himself head of a national church. Across a single generation the seamless robe of Western Christendom was torn into a patchwork of rival confessions.
Here I stand; I can do no other. God help me. Amen.

Luther's defiant words, spoken when ordered to recant before the emperor at the Diet of Worms, became a watchword of the new age — the lone conscience standing against the combined authority of church and crown.
Reform, Counter-Reform and War

The Catholic Church did not stand still. In the **Council of Trent** and the disciplined zeal of new orders like the Jesuits, it mounted a vigorous **Counter-Reformation**, reforming its own abuses while reaffirming the doctrines Protestants had rejected. The contest between the two camps was not only theological. It set off more than a century of brutal religious wars across Europe, culminating in the catastrophic Thirty Years' War, which laid waste much of Germany before exhaustion forced a grudging acceptance that Christendom would remain divided.

body.title: The cost and the gift
body.text: The Reformation cannot be told as a simple tale of liberation. It came with terrible bloodshed, with persecution on every side, with the smashing of [sacred images](/article/christian-art) and the burning of dissenters by Catholics and Protestants alike. Yet its long consequences ran deep and often in directions none of the reformers intended — toward literacy, toward the questioning of inherited authority, and ultimately toward ideas of conscience and self-government that recall the civic spirit of [Greek democracy](/article/greek-democracy).
The Modern World in Embryo

Historians still debate how much of modernity was born in the Reformation, but its fingerprints are everywhere. By insisting that each believer read and judge scripture for themselves, it nourished mass literacy and the habit of private judgement. By breaking the monopoly of a single church, it opened — slowly, painfully, and against the reformers' own intentions — the path toward religious toleration. The sharpened reasoning of [Greek philosophy](/article/greek-philosophy), revived by Renaissance scholars, fed the new readiness to question and to argue. Out of the wreck of medieval unity came a Europe of competing ideas, printed words and individual consciences — the seedbed of the modern mind.

title: The Roman Empire
lede: Out of a century of civil war rose a new order: a single ruler cloaked in republican forms, presiding over an empire that ringed the Mediterranean and, for two unbroken centuries, gave a quarter of humanity the longest peace the ancient world had known.

maps.title: Mare Nostrum — "Our Sea"
maps.caption: At its height the empire encircled the entire Mediterranean, the first and only power ever to hold every shore.

maps.title: The Frontiers
maps.caption: Hadrian's Wall in Britain, the Rhine and Danube in Europe, the desert in Africa and the Euphrates in the east marked the limits of Roman rule.

images.title: The Augustus of Prima Porta
images.caption: The first emperor portrayed as an ageless commander — the carefully crafted image of a man who ruled as "first citizen."

images.title: The Pantheon
images.caption: Hadrian's temple to all the gods, its vast concrete dome still the largest unreinforced dome on Earth after nineteen centuries.

videos.title: A day in imperial Rome
videos.caption: The world's first million-strong city — its insulae, baths, markets and the spectacle of the arena.

citations.label: The British Museum — Roman Empire
citations.source: The British Museum, Department of Greece and Rome

body.text: Augustus and the Republic Restored

When [Julius Caesar](/article/julius-caesar) fell, his eighteen-year-old great-nephew and adopted heir, **Octavian**, inherited his name, his fortune, and the loyalty of his veterans. Over thirteen more years of civil war he destroyed his rivals one by one — last of all Mark Antony and Cleopatra, whose deaths in **30 BCE** delivered [Egypt](/article/ancient-egypt) and its grain into his hands. In **27 BCE** the Senate gave him the name by which history knows him: **Augustus**, "the revered one."

Augustus had watched his adoptive father be murdered for looking like a king, and he did not repeat the mistake. He claimed to have "restored the Republic," kept the old offices and the Senate, and called himself merely *princeps* — first citizen. In reality he held the armies, the treasury, and the provinces that mattered. The Empire was a monarchy wearing the mask of a republic, and the disguise was the point.

body.title: The genius of disguise
body.text: Augustus understood that Romans would accept the substance of one-man rule as long as they were spared its appearance. By preserving republican forms while hollowing out their power, he founded a system stable enough to outlast him by five hundred years. ## The Roman Peace The reward for surrendering political freedom was peace. The **Pax Romana** — roughly **27 BCE to 180 CE** — was two centuries in which the Mediterranean world was, for the only time in its history, united under a single government and largely free of war within its borders. A merchant could travel from Britain to Syria on [Roman roads](/article/roman-engineering), paying in a single currency, speaking Latin in the west and Greek in the east, protected by [the legions](/article/roman-army) on the distant frontiers. > Travel was safe, the seas were cleared of pirates, and a man might journey from one end of the world to the other without fear. Cities flourished as never before. **Rome itself swelled past a million people** — a scale no European city would match again until London in the 1800s. Aqueducts fed its fountains and baths, grain fleets from Egypt and Africa fed its mouths, and the spectacle of the **Colosseum** fed its appetite for blood and wonder. ## The Good, the Mad, and the Wise The emperors were a study in extremes. The first century gave Rome the cold competence of Augustus and Tiberius but also the cruelty of **Nero** and the madness ascribed to **Caligula**. Then came the **Five Good Emperors** — Nerva, Trajan, Hadrian, Antoninus Pius, and the philosopher **Marcus Aurelius** — under whom the empire reached its greatest extent and, many later writers thought, the high-water mark of human government. Under **Trajan** (98–117 CE) the frontiers stretched from the Atlantic to the Persian Gulf. **Hadrian** chose instead to consolidate, walling off the wild north of Britain and fixing the borders he would defend. Marcus Aurelius governed wisely even while writing his private *Meditations* and fighting plague and invasion on the Danube — the model of the reluctant ruler doing his duty. :::note An empire of citizens In 212 CE the emperor Caracalla granted Roman citizenship to nearly every free inhabitant of the empire. The city on the Tiber had finally made its whole world Roman — the culmination of the instinct that had driven [Rome](/article/rome) from the beginning. ## Cracks Beneath the Marble The peace could not hold forever. The empire had no settled rule for succession, so each emperor's death risked civil war; defending thousands of miles of frontier strained the treasury; and in the third century a storm of invasions, plagues, and usurpers nearly tore the whole structure apart. It was rebuilt by hard soldier-emperors like **Diocletian** and **Constantine**, who split the realm to govern it and moved its centre east to a new capital, **Constantinople**. That eastern half would endure for another thousand years. The western half, exhausted and overrun, would not — a long unravelling told in [the fall of Rome](/article/fall-of-rome). But even in decline the empire left the deepest mark any state has left on the world: its law, its language, its roads, and the very idea that a single civilisation might span a continent.

title: The Roman Legions
eyebrow: The machine that built an empire
lede: Disciplined, drilled, and relentlessly organised, the Roman legion was the most effective fighting force of the ancient world — and far more than an army. It was the engineer, road-builder, and frontier of the empire, conquering with the pick and shovel as surely as with the sword.

maps.title: Legions on the Frontier
maps.caption: Permanent fortresses along the Rhine, Danube and in Britain anchored the army to the edges of the empire it defended.

maps.title: The Field of Cannae
maps.caption: Hannibal's annihilation of a far larger Roman army in 216 BCE — the worst defeat Rome ever survived, and learned from.

images.title: Legionary in lorica segmentata
images.caption: The iconic banded iron armour, large rectangular shield (scutum), short sword (gladius) and heavy javelin (pilum) of the imperial soldier.

images.title: Trajan's Column
images.caption: A spiral frieze of 2,600 figures recording the Dacian Wars — the finest visual record of the army at work and war.

videos.title: Anatomy of a legion
videos.caption: How 5,000 men were organised into cohorts and centuries, and why that structure made them so hard to beat.

citations.label: Vegetius — De Re Militari
citations.source: Vegetius, Epitome of Military Science (Liverpool University Press)

citations.label: The British Museum — Roman Army
citations.source: The British Museum, Department of Greece and Rome

body.text: An Army of Citizens, Then of Professionals

In the early [Republic](/article/roman-republic) the army was a militia of citizen-farmers who supplied their own arms and went home after the campaign. This worked while Rome's wars were short and close. But as the empire grew and wars stretched across years and continents, the system buckled. Around **107 BCE** the general **Marius** opened the legions to the landless poor, equipping them at state expense and promising land on discharge.

The change was momentous. The legions became a professional standing army — better trained and more loyal than any militia — but loyal now to the general who paid and led them rather than to the state. That shift armed the ambitions of men like [Julius Caesar](/article/julius-caesar) and helped turn the Republic into the [Empire](/article/roman-empire).
Built for Order

The legion's power lay less in any individual soldier than in its structure. A legion of roughly **5,000 men** was divided into ten **cohorts**, each cohort into six **centuries** of about eighty men, each century led by a **centurion** — the tough career officers who were the backbone of the whole machine. This modular design let commanders manoeuvre blocks of men with a flexibility that the dense, unwieldy phalanxes of their enemies could not match.

body.title: Discipline as a weapon
body.text: What truly set the legions apart was not equipment but discipline. Roman soldiers drilled constantly, obeyed instantly, and faced savage punishment for failure — including *decimation*, the execution of every tenth man in a disgraced unit. A Roman army that broke and ran was rare; one that held its formation could defeat forces many times its size. The soldier carried a large curved shield, the **scutum**; a short stabbing sword, the **gladius**, ideal for the press of close combat; and the **pilum**, a heavy javelin whose soft iron neck bent on impact so it could not be thrown back. Behind the front ranks came artillery — bolt-throwers and stone-hurling catapults that could shatter a wall or a charge. ## The Soldier as Builder Here is the secret most often forgotten: the legionary spent far more days digging than fighting. Every night on campaign the army built a fortified camp to the same exacting plan, ditch and rampart and ordered streets, so that a Roman force was never caught unprepared. In peacetime the same hands built the roads, bridges, walls, and aqueducts of [Roman engineering](/article/roman-engineering) that knit the empire together. > The Romans owe the mastery of the world to military training, to discipline in their camps, and to practice in engineering. An army that could march thirty kilometres in a day and throw up a fort by nightfall was a tool of conquest and administration at once. The frontier was not just a line of soldiers but a line of their stonework — the road they marched and the wall they raised. ## Defeat and Endurance The legions were not invincible. **Hannibal** destroyed army after army in the Punic Wars, annihilating eighty thousand Romans in a single afternoon at **Cannae** in 216 BCE. In **9 CE** three whole legions were ambushed and wiped out in the **Teutoburg Forest** of Germany, a disaster that fixed the empire's northern border at the Rhine forever. What made Rome formidable was not that it never lost but that it could absorb catastrophe and come back. After Cannae it simply raised new armies and ground Hannibal down through sheer persistence. :::caution When the shield became the spear The army that defended Rome could also turn on it. Emperors increasingly owed their thrones to the soldiers, and in the chaotic third century the legions made and murdered rulers at will. The force that built the empire would, in its disorder and its growing reliance on hired barbarian troops, become entangled in [the empire's fall](/article/fall-of-rome).

toc.text: An Army of Citizens, Then of Professionals

title: The Roman Republic
lede: For nearly five centuries Rome governed itself without a monarch, through an intricate balance of magistrates, Senate, and citizen assemblies — a republic that conquered the Mediterranean and then, undone by its own success, gave birth to the empire that replaced it.

maps.title: Rome's Expansion in Italy
maps.caption: From a single city to master of the peninsula by 270 BCE, binding Italian peoples through colonies and alliances.

maps.title: The Punic Wars
maps.caption: A century of struggle with Carthage for control of the western Mediterranean, fought from Sicily to the gates of Rome.

images.title: The Curia Julia
images.caption: The Senate house in the Forum, where Rome's most powerful council debated war, law and finance.

images.title: Bust of Cicero
images.caption: Orator, lawyer and consul — the voice of the Republic's last generation and its constitutional ideals.

videos.title: How the Republic governed itself
videos.caption: Consuls, Senate and assemblies explained — the checks and balances of a constitution without a written constitution.

citations.label: Polybius — The Histories, Book VI
citations.source: Polybius, on the Roman constitution (Loeb Classical Library)

citations.label: The British Museum — Republican Rome
citations.source: The British Museum, Department of Greece and Rome

body.text: The Oath Against Kings

The Republic was born of a refusal. According to tradition, in **509 BCE** the Romans drove out **Tarquin the Proud**, the last of their kings, and bound themselves by oath never to let one man rule alone again. Everything in the new system was designed to keep that oath. Where a king had held power for life, the Republic split his authority among many hands and handed it out for a single year at a time.

The word *res publica* means simply "the public thing" — the commonwealth, the affairs of the people held in common rather than owned by a ruler. It was an idea as much as a government, and the Romans guarded it with a near-religious zeal.
A Machine of Checks and Balances

At the top stood two **consuls**, elected jointly for one year, each able to veto the other so that neither could become a tyrant. Below them ranged a ladder of offices — praetors who judged, quaestors who managed money, aediles who ran the city, censors who counted citizens and policed morals. Climbing this ladder, the *cursus honorum*, was the ambition of every aristocrat.

Behind the magistrates sat the **Senate**, a standing council of several hundred ex-magistrates who held no formal command yet shaped almost everything through sheer authority and experience. And beneath them all stood the **assemblies** of citizens, who elected the magistrates, passed laws, and declared war.

body.title: A constitution of friction
body.text: The Greek historian Polybius marvelled that Rome's system mixed monarchy (the consuls), aristocracy (the Senate), and democracy (the assemblies) so that each restrained the others. Its strength was not efficiency but balance — power was deliberately made hard to seize. ## Patricians, Plebeians, and the Struggle for Rights The early Republic belonged to the **patricians**, a closed circle of noble families who monopolised office and priesthood. The common citizens, the **plebeians**, fought back over two centuries through a remarkable, mostly bloodless weapon: the **secession**. By marching out of the city and refusing to work or fight, the plebs forced concession after concession — their own officials, the **tribunes**, whose persons were sacred and who could veto the acts of magistrates; written law in the **Twelve Tables**; and at last the right to hold the highest offices themselves. > Let justice be done, though the heavens fall. This long "Conflict of the Orders" never abolished inequality, but it wove the plebeians into the state and produced a governing class open enough to renew itself — one more expression of Rome's gift for [absorbing rather than excluding](/article/rome). ## Conquest and the Seeds of Ruin The Republic was a war machine of astonishing endurance. It bound the peoples of Italy into a network of alliances that supplied [soldiers](/article/roman-army) without end, and in three brutal wars with **Carthage** — the Punic Wars — it survived Hannibal's invasion and emerged as the unrivalled power of the western Mediterranean. Greece, Spain, and North Africa followed. Yet conquest carried a poison. Floods of war captives became slaves who worked vast estates, driving small farmers off the land; the spoils of empire made a few families monstrously rich; and victorious generals began to command armies loyal to themselves rather than to Rome. :::caution When the army serves the general, not the state Reforms opened the legions to the landless poor, who now looked to their commander for land and pay. An ambitious general with a devoted army became a power the Senate could not control — the fatal flaw that would destroy the Republic. ## The Long Collapse The Republic's last century was a slow-motion suicide: the murdered reformers **Tiberius and Gaius Gracchus**, the rival warlords **Marius and Sulla**, civil wars fought in the streets of Rome itself. The system that had been built to stop any one man from ruling could no longer stop the men its conquests had created. The crisis found its resolution in [Julius Caesar](/article/julius-caesar), and through his heir Augustus passed quietly into the [Roman Empire](/article/roman-empire). The Republic was never formally abolished — its offices and Senate survived for centuries — but the public thing had become, in truth, one man's.

title: The Solar System
lede: One ordinary star, eight planets, scores of moons, and countless lumps of rock and ice — all bound together by gravity and born from a single collapsing cloud 4.6 billion years ago. The Solar System is the small, well-lit neighbourhood that humanity calls home.

maps.title: The layout of the Solar System
maps.caption: The Sun, the four rocky inner planets, the asteroid belt, the four giant outer planets, and the icy realms beyond.

maps.title: The Sun's reach
maps.caption: The heliosphere — the vast bubble of solar wind that marks the boundary between the Sun's domain and interstellar space.

images.title: The Sun, our anchor
images.caption: Holding 99.86 percent of all the mass in the Solar System, the Sun's gravity governs everything that orbits it.

images.title: The family portrait
images.caption: A pale blue dot — Earth seen by Voyager 1 from beyond Neptune, a mote of dust in a sunbeam.

images.title: The protoplanetary disk
images.caption: A young star ringed by the dusty disk in which planets coalesce, much as ours did 4.6 billion years ago.

videos.title: How the Solar System formed
videos.caption: From a spinning cloud of gas and dust to a star with a family of worlds.

videos.title: A tour from the Sun to the Oort Cloud
videos.caption: Crossing the true scale of the Solar System, from the inner planets to its distant icy edge.

citations.label: NASA — How the Solar System formed

body.text: Born from a Cloud

About 4.6 billion years ago, in an unremarkable part of the Milky Way, a giant cloud of gas and dust — perhaps nudged by the shockwave of a nearby [supernova](/article/stars) — began to collapse under its own gravity. As it fell inward it spun faster and flattened into a swirling disk, with most of the material piling up at the centre.

When that central mass grew hot and dense enough, fusion ignited and the **Sun** switched on. The leftover material in the surrounding disk clumped together grain by grain, building first into pebbles, then boulders, then worlds. Everything in the Solar System — every planet, moon, asteroid, and comet — condensed from that same primordial disk, which is why they all orbit in roughly the same plane and the same direction to this day.

body.title: One star, almost all the mass
body.text: The Sun contains about 99.86 percent of all the mass in the Solar System. Everything else — all eight planets, every moon, and every asteroid combined — makes up barely a tenth of one percent. The Solar System is, overwhelmingly, the Sun plus some rounding error.
A System in Two Halves

The Solar System divides neatly into an inner and an outer region, separated by the asteroid belt. The split is a direct legacy of how it formed: close to the young Sun it was too hot for ices to survive, so only rock and metal could condense, while farther out, beyond the **frost line**, ice was abundant and worlds could grow huge.

items: **The inner Solar System** holds the four small, rocky **terrestrial planets** — Mercury, Venus, Earth, and Mars — dense balls of rock and iron.
**The asteroid belt** lies between Mars and Jupiter: a band of rocky debris, the rubble of a planet that never formed, kept stirred up by Jupiter's gravity.

**The outer Solar System** is the realm of the **giant planets** — Jupiter and Saturn, the gas giants, and Uranus and Neptune, the ice giants — each far larger than Earth and wreathed in moons and rings.

body.text: These worlds, and what makes each one distinct, are the subject of [the planets](/article/the-planets).
The Sun: Engine and Anchor

The **Sun** is the heart of it all. Its gravity holds every planet in its orbit, and its light and warmth drive the weather, the seasons, and almost all life on Earth. A stream of charged particles called the **solar wind** blows constantly outward, carving a vast protective bubble — the **heliosphere** — around the entire planetary system.

The Sun is a middling [star](/article/stars), roughly halfway through a ten-billion-year life. For now it is remarkably steady, but it is not eternal. In several billion years it will swell into a red giant, scorching the inner planets and reshaping the Solar System for its final act — a long-term fate traced in [the future of space](/article/the-future-of-space).
Moons, Rings, and Rubble
Planets are only the beginning. The Solar System teems with smaller bodies, many of them worlds in their own right.

More than **two hundred moons** orbit the planets, and some are astonishing places: Jupiter's **Europa** and Saturn's **Enceladus** hide oceans of liquid water beneath shells of ice; Saturn's **Titan** has rivers and lakes of liquid methane under a thick orange haze; and Earth's own [Moon](/article/the-moon) shaped the conditions for life on our planet.

Then there are the wanderers. **Asteroids** are rocky leftovers, concentrated in the belt but scattered widely. **Comets** are icy bodies that grow glowing tails when the Sun's heat boils off their surface, swinging in from the cold outer reaches on long, looping orbits.

body.title: Why Pluto was reclassified
body.text: In 2006 the International Astronomical Union defined a "planet" as a body that, among other things, has cleared its orbital neighbourhood of other objects. Pluto shares its region with many similar icy bodies, so it was reclassified as a **dwarf planet** — a category it shares with Ceres, Eris, and others. It was not a demotion so much as a recognition that Pluto belongs to a whole new population of worlds.
The Distant Edges

Far beyond Neptune lies the **Kuiper Belt**, a broad ring of icy bodies — Pluto among them — that are frozen relics of the Solar System's birth. Farther still, perhaps a thousand times more distant, astronomers believe a vast spherical shell of icy debris called the **Oort Cloud** surrounds the whole system, marking the true outer boundary of the Sun's gravitational reach. It is so remote that a comet from there can take millions of years to complete a single orbit.

The scale is hard to grasp. If the Sun were the size of a beach ball, Earth would be a peppercorn some 25 metres away, and the edge of the Oort Cloud would lie kilometres distant. The Solar System is mostly empty space.
Reaching Beyond

Only one human-made object has crossed the heliosphere into interstellar space: **Voyager 1**, launched in 1977, which slipped past the Sun's bubble in 2012 and now drifts among the stars, still faintly calling home. Its journey is the opening chapter of humanity's reach beyond our own neighbourhood — a story continued in [space missions](/article/space-missions).

For all our exploration, the Solar System remains the only place we have ever directly visited, the only star we have studied up close, and — so far — the only place known to harbour life. Understanding it is how we calibrate everything else: a single, well-mapped example against which we measure the billions of other planetary systems now being discovered across the galaxy.

title: The Sunni-Shia Divide
lede: The great division of Islam between Sunni and Shia did not begin over doctrine but over a question of leadership — who should guide the community after the Prophet Muhammad. From that early disagreement grew two distinct traditions, each with its own history, law, ritual and memory, together making up the whole of the Muslim world. Understanding the split is to understand much of Islamic history, and to see how a single faith can hold within it two profoundly different ways of remembering its own beginnings.

maps.title: The Geography of the Divide
maps.caption: Sunnis make up the great majority of the Muslim world, while Shia communities are concentrated in Iran, Iraq, Bahrain, Azerbaijan and parts of Lebanon, the Gulf and South Asia.

images.title: The shrine of Imam Husayn at Karbala
images.caption: The tomb of the Prophet's grandson, killed in 680, is among the holiest sites of Shia Islam — the focus of the annual mourning of Ashura.

images.title: Pilgrims at Ashura
images.caption: Each year Shia Muslims commemorate the martyrdom of Husayn at Karbala with processions of mourning — a remembrance at the heart of Shia identity.

videos.title: Why Islam Divided
videos.caption: The question of succession after Muhammad, and how it grew into the two great branches of Islam.

body.text: A Question of Succession

When [Muhammad](/article/muhammad) died in the year 632, he left behind a flourishing community but no agreed plan for who should lead it. He had founded a faith, a state and a way of life — but the [Quran](/article/the-quran) did not name a successor, and the Prophet's own intentions were remembered differently by different people. Out of that single silence grew the deepest division in the history of Islam. It was not, at first, a quarrel about belief. Both sides held the same creed, prayed the same prayers, revered the same book. They differed over a question of authority: who had the right to lead the **umma**, the community of the faithful, in the Prophet's place.

One group held that the leader — the **caliph**, or "successor" — should be the most capable and pious man, chosen by the consensus of the community's elders. They accepted the succession of Muhammad's close companions: first **Abu Bakr**, the Prophet's old friend and father-in-law, then Umar, then Uthman, and finally Ali. These became known as the **Sunni**, from *ahl al-sunna*, "the people of the tradition" — those who follow the practice of the Prophet and the consensus of the community. Today they are the great majority, perhaps nine in ten of all Muslims.

The other group held that leadership belonged by right to the Prophet's own family — specifically to **Ali ibn Abi Talib**, Muhammad's cousin and son-in-law, and to Ali's descendants after him. They believed Muhammad had designated Ali as his heir, and that spiritual authority passed through this bloodline alone. These became the **Shia**, from *shiʿat Ali*, "the party of Ali."

body.title: A divide of history, not of God
body.text: It is worth saying clearly: Sunni and Shia are both fully Muslim. They share the same God, the same Prophet, the same Quran, the same Five Pillars, the same direction of prayer toward Mecca. Their division is rooted in history — in who should have led, and in the events that followed — far more than in the fundamentals of belief. Across most of Islamic history Sunni and Shia have lived side by side, intermarried and shared cities. The bitter sectarian conflicts of modern times are often as much about politics and power as about the ancient theological difference, much as the wars of the European [Reformation](/article/reformation) were never about doctrine alone.
Karbala and the Birth of Shia Memory

The division might have remained a quiet difference of opinion but for a tragedy that seared itself into Shia memory forever. Ali did at last become the fourth caliph, but his rule was contested and he was assassinated in 661, after which power passed to the **Umayyad** dynasty, whom the Shia regarded as usurpers. In 680, Ali's son **Husayn** — the Prophet's own grandson — rose against the Umayyad caliph and was surrounded with his small band of followers on the plain of **Karbala**, in what is now Iraq. There, cut off from water and hopelessly outnumbered, Husayn and nearly all his companions were killed.

The martyrdom of Husayn at Karbala became the defining event of Shia Islam — a story of righteous suffering against worldly injustice that Shia Muslims relive every year in the mourning rituals of **Ashura**, with processions, weeping and passion plays. Where Sunni Islam tended to make its peace with whatever ruler held power, Shia Islam carried forward a powerful sense of righteous resistance to unjust authority, born at Karbala.
Every day is Ashura, and every land is Karbala.
That Shia saying captures how the memory of Husayn's sacrifice became a permanent moral lens — every struggle against injustice an echo of that first one.
Two Traditions Take Shape

Over the centuries the two branches developed into fully distinct traditions. The Shia came to venerate a line of **Imams** — for the largest Shia group, the "Twelvers," a succession of twelve divinely guided descendants of Ali, the last of whom they believe did not die but went into a hidden state, the **occultation**, and will return at the end of time as the **Mahdi** to fill the world with justice. This gave Shia Islam a distinct religious leadership of learned clerics who interpret the faith in the Imam's absence — a structured hierarchy with no real equivalent in Sunni Islam.

The Sunni, for their part, developed their authority through the consensus of scholars (*ulama*) and the great schools of religious law, grounding the faith in the Quran and the collected traditions of the Prophet. Each branch produced its own collections of hadith, its own schools of jurisprudence, its own theologians and saints. Both shared fully in the brilliance of the [Islamic Golden Age](/article/islamic-golden-age); Shia dynasties such as the Fatimids of Egypt founded great cities and centres of learning, and the divide ran through scholarship and philosophy as through everything else.

body.title: An old pattern of faith
body.text: The shape of this division — a community splitting over who holds rightful authority after the founder, and over how that authority is transmitted — is one religions know well. The [early Christian church](/article/early-church) wrestled for centuries over the authority of bishops and the see of Rome, divisions that hardened into the lasting split between East and West. In both faiths the deepest fractures grew not from disagreement about God but from the human question of who speaks for Him. To trace the Sunni–Shia divide is to watch a universal drama of religious history play out in Islam's own distinctive key.
The Divide in the Modern World

Today Sunnis make up roughly 85 to 90 percent of the world's Muslims, with Shia communities concentrated in Iran — where Twelver Shiism is the state religion — and in Iraq, Bahrain, Azerbaijan, Lebanon and parts of the Gulf and South Asia. For most of history the two have coexisted far more often than they have fought, sharing the same lands and the same daily faith. But in the modern era the old religious difference has at times been inflamed by political rivalry between states and movements, turning an ancient theological distinction into a marker of contemporary conflict.

To understand [Islam today](/article/islam-today), one must hold both truths together: that the divide is real and runs deep into the earliest days of the faith, and that what unites Sunni and Shia — the one God, the one Prophet, the one Quran, the one direction of prayer — is far greater than what divides them. The split that began with a question of who should lead has shaped fourteen centuries of history, yet it has never broken the underlying unity of a faith now shared by nearly two billion souls.

title: The Trojan War
lede: A face that launched a thousand ships, a wooden horse, a city burned for the love of a queen — the Trojan War is the founding story of Greek literature. Whether it ever truly happened, and how much of Homer's epic is memory and how much is myth, remains one of history's most haunting questions.

maps.title: Troy and the Aegean
maps.caption: The site of Hisarlik on the coast of Asia Minor, commanding the strait between the Aegean and the Black Sea — the likely Troy.

images.title: The Trojan Horse
images.caption: The wooden horse left as a false offering, its belly full of hidden warriors — the ruse that took the city.

images.title: The Mask of Agamemnon
images.caption: A golden funeral mask from Mycenae, once wrongly thought to be the face of the legendary Greek king.

videos.title: Did the Trojan War happen?
videos.caption: Weighing Homer's epic against the archaeology of Hisarlik.

citations.label: The British Museum — Troy: Myth and Reality
citations.source: The British Museum, Department of Greece and Rome

body.text: The Quarrel That Began It

The story the Greeks told was this. At a wedding of the gods, the goddess of discord threw down a golden apple marked "for the fairest," and three goddesses — Hera, Athena, and Aphrodite — fell to quarrelling over it. They asked a Trojan prince, **Paris**, to judge, and **Aphrodite** won his vote by promising him the most beautiful woman in the world. That woman was **Helen**, wife of the Greek king Menelaus of Sparta. Paris carried her off to Troy, and all the kings of Greece, bound by an oath, gathered a vast fleet under Menelaus's brother **Agamemnon** to win her back.

For ten years the Greek host besieged the great walled city of **Troy** on the coast of Asia Minor, and could not break it. The *Iliad* of **Homer**, the foundation poem of Western literature, tells only a few weeks of that final year, centred on the towering rage of the Greek hero **Achilles** — his quarrel with Agamemnon, his withdrawal from the fighting, the death of his friend Patroclus, and his terrible revenge on the Trojan champion **Hector**.

body.title: A poem about anger, not victory
body.text: The Iliad does not end with Troy's fall. It is not really about the war at all but about wrath, grief, and mortality — the price heroes pay for glory. Homer made the founding war of his people a meditation on the human cost of pride. ## The Wooden Horse The city fell at last not by force but by cunning. The Greeks built an enormous hollow **wooden horse**, hid their best warriors inside it, and sailed away as if defeated, leaving the horse as a supposed offering to the gods. The Trojans, against the warnings of those who smelled a trap, dragged it within their walls in triumph. That night the hidden Greeks crept out, opened the gates to their returned comrades, and put Troy to the sword. > I fear the Greeks, even bearing gifts. The Trojan priest Laocoön's warning, immortalised later in Latin, has become a proverb for the danger hidden inside a tempting offer. The fall of Troy — the burning city, the slaughter, the queens led off into slavery — became one of the most powerful images in all of [Greek mythology](/article/greek-mythology), a byword for the ruin that pride and the gods can bring upon even the mightiest. ## Memory or Myth? For most of history the Trojan War was taken as real, then dismissed as pure legend. Then in the 1870s a German businessman named **Heinrich Schliemann**, clutching his Homer, dug into the mound of **Hisarlik** in north-western Turkey and found not one city but the layered ruins of many, one atop another across thousands of years. He had found *a* Troy — a real city, repeatedly destroyed and rebuilt, in just the place Homer described, commanding the rich trade route to the Black Sea. :::caution Why this story carries a "theory" badge That a city stood at Troy is certain. That it was besieged and burned around 1200 BCE is likely. But whether a great coalition of Greeks fought there for a stolen queen, whether Achilles or Helen ever lived, and how much of Homer is history rather than centuries of poetic invention — none of that can be proven. The war hovers between archaeology and art. Archaeology has shown that the late **Bronze Age** world Homer describes was real: the gold-rich **Mycenaean** kingdoms of Greece, the powerful citadel of Troy, the great powers like the Hittites and [Egypt](/article/ancient-egypt) whose records mention raiders and wars along these coasts. A real conflict, or a dim memory of many, may well lie behind the legend, polished by four centuries of oral poetry before Homer fixed it in verse. ## The War That Outlived Troy Whatever its truth, the story refused to die. The Greeks treated the *Iliad* almost as scripture; [Alexander the Great](/article/alexander-the-great) is said to have slept with a copy beneath his pillow and to have visited the supposed tomb of Achilles. And when [Rome](/article/roman-mythology) sought a noble origin for itself, it claimed descent from the Trojan prince **Aeneas**, who in Virgil's *Aeneid* escapes the burning city to found the line of Rome — turning Troy's defeat into the seed of a greater empire. So the Trojan War became less an event than a story the West has told and retold for three thousand years — about beauty and war, honour and ruin, the glory of heroes and the terrible cost of their pride. It may be the most influential war that we are not even sure took place.

title: The Vedas
lede: The Vedas are the oldest scriptures of Hinduism and among the oldest sacred texts of any living religion — a vast body of hymns, chants and ritual formulas composed in archaic Sanskrit more than three thousand years ago and preserved, with astonishing exactness, by memory alone for a thousand years before they were ever written down.

maps.title: The Land of the Seven Rivers
maps.caption: The northwestern plains where the Vedic peoples composed the Rigveda, a world of rivers, cattle and fire-altars later remembered as the cradle of the tradition.

images.title: A palm-leaf Rigveda manuscript
images.caption: For most of their history the Vedas were never written at all — when finally inscribed, the text only confirmed what reciters had carried flawlessly in memory.

images.title: The fire sacrifice
images.caption: Agni, the fire, carried the offerings of the Vedic ritual up to the gods — a ceremony still performed by Brahmin priests today.

videos.title: The oldest scriptures alive
videos.caption: How an entire library was preserved for a millennium without a single written word.

citations.label: The Rigveda
citations.source: The Rigveda (trans. Wendy Doniger, Penguin Classics)

body.text: The Knowledge That Was Heard

The word *veda* means simply **knowledge** — and to the tradition this was no ordinary knowledge but truth itself, not composed by human authors but *heard* by ancient seers, the **rishis**, in states of heightened vision. For this reason the Vedas are called **shruti**, "that which was heard," and they hold a unique authority: they are eternal, uncreated, the very sound of reality, transmitted through the rishis rather than invented by them.

The oldest and greatest of them is the **Rigveda**, a collection of more than a thousand hymns to the gods, likely taking shape around 1500 BCE among the Sanskrit-speaking peoples of the northwestern plains. Three further collections grew alongside it: the **Samaveda**, the same verses arranged as melody for chanting; the **Yajurveda**, the prose formulas a priest murmured as he performed the sacrifice; and the **Atharvaveda**, a more earthy gathering of spells, blessings and charms for healing and daily life. Together they form a complete liturgy for a religion built around the sacred fire.

body.title: A library carried in the mind
body.text: For perhaps a thousand years the Vedas were never written down — and were never meant to be. They were preserved by memory alone, recited by Brahmin families who learned not only the words but elaborate redundant chanting patterns that crisscrossed each syllable forwards, backwards and interwoven, so that no single error could survive. The result is one of the most accurate acts of transmission in human history: scholars believe the text we hear today is essentially identical to what was chanted three thousand years ago. UNESCO has recognised this living chant as a masterpiece of the heritage of humanity.
A World of Fire and Sacrifice

The religion of the Rigveda was vivid and elemental. Its gods were powers of the natural and moral world: **Agni**, the fire that carried offerings skyward; **Indra**, the storm-king who slew the serpent of drought and released the waters; **Varuna**, the stern guardian of cosmic order; **Surya**, the sun; **Ushas**, the radiant dawn. Worship centred on the **yajna**, the fire sacrifice, in which offerings were poured into the flames so that the gods might in turn grant rain, cattle, sons and victory. It was a religion of exchange and order, and it bears a family resemblance to the open-air ritual religions of the wider ancient world — the bargains with the gods kept by [Rome](/article/roman-religion) and the older reasoning seekers of [Greece](/article/greek-philosophy) who would, far to the west, begin their own search for first principles.
There was neither non-existence nor existence then; there was neither the realm of space nor the sky beyond. What stirred? Where? In whose protection?

That hymn — the famous *Nasadiya Sukta*, the Hymn of Creation — shows the Vedas already reaching past ritual toward philosophy, daring even to wonder whether the gods themselves know how the world began.
From Ritual to Insight

Each Veda grew over centuries into layers. After the hymns came the **Brahmanas**, dense manuals explaining the meaning of every ritual gesture, and the **Aranyakas**, the "forest texts" for hermits who had withdrawn from society. Finally, crowning the whole structure, came the **Upanishads** — and here the religion turned inward. Where the early hymns sought rain and cattle from the gods outside, the Upanishads sought the deathless reality within, asking what lies behind the sacrifice, behind the self, behind the cosmos. They are called the **Vedanta**, "the end of the Vedas," and from their meditations grew the whole tradition of [Hindu philosophy](/article/hindu-philosophy).

body.title: Why the Vedas still matter
body.text: A modern Hindu may never read the Rigveda, yet its presence is everywhere. The sacred syllable **Om**, the fire still lit at weddings, the **Gayatri** verse murmured at dawn, the very claim that there is one truth behind many gods — all descend from these hymns. To call a teaching *Vedic* is still, after three thousand years, to call it true. The Vedas are not a closed museum of an ancient faith but the root from which a living tradition continues to draw.
The Root and the Tree

It is hard to overstate what the Vedas began. They gave Hinduism its sacred language, Sanskrit; its central ritual, the fire offering; its first gods and its first questions. They established the idea of revelation as something heard rather than written, and the practice of guarding truth through flawless recitation. And in their final pages, in the soaring speculation of the Upanishads, they planted the seed of every later flowering — the [pantheon](/article/hindu-gods) that would grow ever richer, the epics that would carry the teaching to ordinary people, and the philosophy that would spend the next two thousand years unfolding a single overwhelming intuition: that the self and the cosmos are, at the deepest level, one.
